From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Mother of Jesus

Several terms redirect here. For other uses, see [Mary, Mother of Jesus (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mary,_Mother_of_Jesus_(disambiguation) "Mary, Mother of Jesus (disambiguation)"), [Mary of Nazareth (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mary_of_Nazareth_(disambiguation) "Mary of Nazareth (disambiguation)"), [Mother Mary (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mother_Mary_(disambiguation) "Mother Mary (disambiguation)"), [Saint Mary (disambiguation)](/wiki/Saint_Mary_(disambiguation) "Saint Mary (disambiguation)"), and [Virgin Mary (disambiguation)](/wiki/Virgin_Mary_(disambiguation) "Virgin Mary (disambiguation)").

**Mary**[[b]](#cite_note-10) was a first-century [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") woman of [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth"),[[9]](#cite_note-11) the wife of [Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph") and the mother of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"). She is an important figure of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), venerated under [various titles](/wiki/Titles_of_Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Titles of Mary, mother of Jesus") such as [virgin](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary") or [queen](/wiki/Queen_of_Heaven "Queen of Heaven"), many of which are mentioned in the [Litany of Loreto](/wiki/Litany_of_Loreto "Litany of Loreto"). The [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Evangelical Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") and [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") churches believe that Mary, as mother of Jesus, is the [Mother of God](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"). The [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") historically regarded her as [Christotokos](/wiki/Christotokos "Christotokos"), a term still used in the liturgy of the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East").[[10]](#cite_note-12) She has the [highest position in Islam](/wiki/Mary_in_Islam "Mary in Islam") among all women and is mentioned numerous times in the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), including in a chapter (surah) [named after her](/wiki/Maryam_(surah) "Maryam (surah)").[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-Jestice-2004-15) She is also revered in the [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD "Baháʼí") and [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") faiths.[[14]](#cite_note-Bowman-2012-16)

The [synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels") name Mary as the mother of Jesus. The gospels of [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") describe Mary as a virgin[[c]](#cite_note-20) who was chosen by [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") to [conceive Jesus](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") through the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"). After [giving birth to Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"), she and her husband Joseph raised him in the city of Nazareth in [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee"), and she was in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") at his [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") and with the [apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament") after his [ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus"). Although her later life is not accounted in the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"); Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, and some [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") traditions believe that her body [was raised](/wiki/Entering_heaven_alive "Entering heaven alive") into [heaven](/wiki/Heaven_in_Christianity "Heaven in Christianity") at the end of her earthly life, which is known in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") as the [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") and in [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") as the [Dormition of the Mother of God](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Mother_of_God "Dormition of the Mother of God").

[Mary has been venerated](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary "Veneration of Mary") since [early Christianity](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity"),[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22) and is often considered to be the holiest and greatest [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint"). There is a certain diversity in the [Mariology](/wiki/Mariology "Mariology") and devotional practices of major Christian traditions. The Catholic Church and some Oriental Orthodox Churches hold distinctive Marian [dogmas](/wiki/Dogma_in_the_Catholic_Church#Dogma_as_divine_and_Catholic_faith "Dogma in the Catholic Church"), namely her [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception") and her bodily assumption into heaven.[[20]](#cite_note-23) Many [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") hold [various views of Mary's role](/wiki/Protestant_views_on_Mary "Protestant views on Mary") that they perceive as being in accordance with the Scriptures.[[21]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2004-24)[[22]](#cite_note-Wengert-2017-25) The [Confessions](/wiki/Book_of_Concord "Book of Concord") of the [Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") have taught the [virgin birth](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus"), [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"), and [perpetual virginity](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary").[[23]](#cite_note-Smith-2010-26)[[24]](#cite_note-Anderson-1992-27)

The multiple forms of [Marian devotions](/wiki/Marian_devotions "Marian devotions") include various [prayers](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") and [hymns](/wiki/Hymns_to_Mary "Hymns to Mary"), the celebration of several [Marian feast days](/wiki/Marian_feast_days "Marian feast days") in [liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy"),[[22]](#cite_note-Wengert-2017-25) the veneration of [images](/wiki/Religious_image "Religious image") and [relics](/wiki/Relics "Relics"), the construction of [churches dedicated to her](/wiki/Catholic_Marian_church_buildings "Catholic Marian church buildings") and [pilgrimages](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage") to [Marian shrines](/wiki/Shrines_to_the_Virgin_Mary "Shrines to the Virgin Mary"). Many [Marian apparitions](/wiki/Marian_apparition "Marian apparition") and [miracles](/wiki/Miracle "Miracle") attributed to her [intercession](/wiki/Intercession_of_saints "Intercession of saints") have been reported by believers over the centuries. She has been a traditional [subject in arts](/wiki/Madonna_(art) "Madonna (art)"), notably in [Byzantine art](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art"), [medieval art](/wiki/Medieval_art "Medieval art") and [Renaissance art](/wiki/Renaissance_art "Renaissance art").

Mary's name in the original manuscripts of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") was based on her original [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic_language "Aramaic language") name מרים, transliterated as *[Maryam](/wiki/Maryam_(name) "Maryam (name)")* or *Mariam*.[[25]](#cite_note-28) The English name *[Mary](/wiki/Mary_(name) "Mary (name)")* comes from the Greek Μαρία, a shortened form of the name Μαριάμ. Both Μαρία and Μαριάμ appear in the New Testament.

### In Christianity

In Christianity, Mary is commonly referred to as the Virgin Mary, in accordance with the belief that the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") impregnated her, thereby conceiving her first-born son Jesus [miraculously](/wiki/Miraculous_births "Miraculous births"), without sexual relations with her betrothed Joseph, "until her son [Jesus] was born".[[26]](#cite_note-29) The word "until" has inspired considerable analysis on whether Joseph and Mary produced [siblings](/wiki/Brothers_of_Jesus "Brothers of Jesus") after the birth of Jesus or not.[[d]](#cite_note-31) Among her many other names and titles are the [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church") (often abbreviated to "BVM" after the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *Beata Maria Virgo*),[[28]](#cite_note-32) [Saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint") Mary (occasionally), the [Mother of God](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") (primarily in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity")), the *[Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos")* (primarily in [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity")), Our Lady (Medieval [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Madonna*), and [Queen of Heaven](/wiki/Queen_of_Heaven "Queen of Heaven") (*Regina caeli*; see also [here](/wiki/Regina_Coeli_(disambiguation) "Regina Coeli (disambiguation)")).[[29]](#cite_note-33)[[30]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2003-34) The title "[queen of heaven](/wiki/Queen_of_heaven_(antiquity) "Queen of heaven (antiquity)")" had previously been used as an [epithet](/wiki/Epithet "Epithet") for a number of goddesses, such as [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"), or [Ishtar](/wiki/Ishtar "Ishtar").

Titles in use vary among [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglican_Marian_theology "Anglican Marian theology"), [Lutherans](/wiki/Luther%27s_Marian_theology "Luther's Marian theology") and other [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant_views_on_Mary "Protestant views on Mary"), as well as [Mormons](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_views_on_Mary "Latter Day Saint views on Mary"), [Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Mariology "Roman Catholic Mariology"), [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Marian_theology "Orthodox Marian theology") and [other Christians](/wiki/Ecumenical_meetings_and_documents_on_Mary "Ecumenical meetings and documents on Mary").

The three main titles for Mary used by the Orthodox are *Theotokos* (Θεοτόκος or "God-bearer"), *[Aeiparthenos](/wiki/Aeiparthenos "Aeiparthenos")* (ἀειπαρθένος) which means ever-virgin, as confirmed in the [Second Council of Constantinople](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Constantinople "Second Council of Constantinople") in 553, and *[Panagia](/wiki/Panagia "Panagia")* (Παναγία) meaning "all-holy".[[31]](#cite_note-Fairbairn-2002-35) Catholics use a wide variety of titles for Mary, and these titles have in turn given rise to many artistic depictions.

The title *Theotokos*, which means "God-bearer", was recognized at the [Council of Ephesus](/wiki/Council_of_Ephesus "Council of Ephesus") in 431.[[32]](#cite_note-36)[[33]](#cite_note-37) The direct equivalents of title in Latin are *Deipara* and *Dei Genitrix*, although the phrase is more often loosely translated into Latin as *Mater Dei* ("Mother of God"), with similar patterns for other languages used in the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"). However, this same phrase in Greek (Μήτηρ Θεοῦ), in the abbreviated form ΜΡ ΘΥ, is an indication commonly attached to her image in [Byzantine](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon"). The Council stated that the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") "did not hesitate to speak of the holy Virgin as the Mother of God".[[34]](#cite_note-ccel.org-38)[[35]](#cite_note-39)[[36]](#cite_note-40)

Some Marian titles have a direct [scriptural](/wiki/Bible "Bible") basis. For instance, the title "Queen Mother" has been given to Mary, as she was the mother of Jesus, sometimes referred to as the "King of Kings" due to his ancestral descent from [King David](/wiki/David "David").[[37]](#cite_note-41)[[38]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-43)[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45) This is also based on the Hebrew tradition of the "Queen-Mother", the [Gebirah](/wiki/Gebirah "Gebirah") or "Great Lady".[[42]](#cite_note-46)[[43]](#cite_note-47) Other titles have arisen from [reported miracles](/wiki/Miracle "Miracle"), special appeals, or occasions for calling on Mary.[[e]](#cite_note-52)

### In Islam

Main article: [Mary in Islam](/wiki/Mary_in_Islam "Mary in Islam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Virgin_Mary_Painting_by_Hossein_Nuri.jpg/250px-Virgin_Mary_Painting_by_Hossein_Nuri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virgin_Mary_Painting_by_Hossein_Nuri.jpg)

*Virgin Mary* depicted by Muslim painter [Hossein Nuri](/wiki/Hossein_Nuri#Virgin_Mary_painting "Hossein Nuri")

In [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), Mary is known as [Maryam](/wiki/Mary_in_Islam "Mary in Islam") ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): مريم, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Maryam*), mother of [Isa](/wiki/Jesus_in_Islam "Jesus in Islam") (عيسى بن مريم, *ʿĪsā ibn Maryām*, lit. 'Jesus, son of Mary'). She is often referred to by the honorific title *"Sayyidatuna"*, meaning "Our Lady"; this title is in parallel to *"Sayyiduna"* ("Our Lord"), used for the prophets.[[48]](#cite_note-53) A related term of endearment is *"Siddiqah"*,[[49]](#cite_note-54) meaning "she who confirms the truth" and "she who believes sincerely completely". Another title for Mary is *"Qānitah"*, which signifies both constant submission to God and absorption in prayer and invocation in Islam.[[50]](#cite_note-qref|66|12|b=y-55) She is also called *"Tahira"*, meaning "one who has been purified" and representing her status as one of two humans in creation to not be touched by [Satan](/wiki/Iblis "Iblis") at any point, the other being Jesus.[[51]](#cite_note-qref|3|36|b=y-56) In the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), she is described both as "the daughter of Imran" and "the sister of Aaron" and Moses, alluding to [Miriam](/wiki/Miriam "Miriam") from the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible").[[52]](#cite_note-57) However, the title of "the sister of Aaron" is confirmed to be metaphorical (which is a common [figure of speech](/wiki/Figure_of_speech "Figure of speech") in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic")) as per a [Hadith](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith") from the [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") explaining Mary was indeed named after Miriam.[[53]](#cite_note-58)

## Life in ancient sources

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Eustache_Le_Sueur.jpg/250px-Eustache_Le_Sueur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eustache_Le_Sueur.jpg)

*[The Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation")* by [Eustache Le Sueur](/wiki/Eustache_Le_Sueur "Eustache Le Sueur"), an example of 17th century [Marian art](/wiki/Marian_art_in_the_Catholic_Church "Marian art in the Catholic Church"). The [Angel Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel") announces to Mary her pregnancy with Jesus and offers her [white lilies](/wiki/Lilium_candidum "Lilium candidum").

### New Testament

The [canonical Gospels](/wiki/Canonical_Gospels "Canonical Gospels") and the [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles") are the [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source") of historical information about Mary.[[54]](#cite_note-Hesemann-2016-59)[[55]](#cite_note-Canales-2010-60) They are almost contemporary sources, as the [synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels") and the Acts of the Apostles are generally considered dating from around AD 66–90, while the [gospel of John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") dates from AD 90–110. They provide limited information about Mary, as they primarily focus on the teaching of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") and on [his apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament").[[54]](#cite_note-Hesemann-2016-59) The [historical reliability of the Gospels](/wiki/Historical_reliability_of_the_Gospels "Historical reliability of the Gospels") and [historical reliability of the Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Historical_reliability_of_the_Acts_of_the_Apostles "Historical reliability of the Acts of the Apostles") are subject to debate, as it was common practice in early Christian writings to mix historical facts with legendary stories.[[54]](#cite_note-Hesemann-2016-59)

The earliest New Testament account of Mary is in the [epistle to the Galatians](/wiki/Epistle_to_the_Galatians "Epistle to the Galatians"), which was written before the [gospels](/wiki/Gospels "Gospels"). She is referred to as "a woman" and is not named: "But when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son, born of a woman, born under the law" (Galatians 4:4).[[55]](#cite_note-Canales-2010-60)

Mary is mentioned several times in the canonical Gospels and the Acts of the Apostles:

* The [Gospel of Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") mentions Mary the most often, identifying her by name twelve times, all of these in the infancy narrative (Luke 1:27–2:34).[[56]](#cite_note-61)
* The [Gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") mentions her by name five times, four of these (1:16, 18, 20; 2:11)[[57]](#cite_note-62) in the infancy narrative and once (Matthew 13:55)[[58]](#cite_note-63) outside the infancy narrative.
* The [Gospel of Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark") names her once (Mark 6:3)[[59]](#cite_note-64) and mentions Jesus' mother without naming her in Mark 3:31–32.[[60]](#cite_note-65)
* The [Gospel of John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") refers to the mother of Jesus twice, but never mentions her name. She is first seen at the [wedding at Cana](/wiki/Marriage_at_Cana "Marriage at Cana") (John 2:1–12).[[61]](#cite_note-John_2-66) The second reference has her standing near the cross of Jesus together with [Mary Magdalene](/wiki/Mary_Magdalene "Mary Magdalene"), [Mary of Clopas](/wiki/Mary_of_Clopas "Mary of Clopas") (or Cleophas), and her own sister (possibly the same as Mary of Clopas; the wording is semantically ambiguous), along with the "[disciple whom Jesus loved](/wiki/Disciple_whom_Jesus_loved "Disciple whom Jesus loved")" (John 19:25–26).[[62]](#cite_note-John_19-67) John 2:1–12[[61]](#cite_note-John_2-66) is the only text in the canonical gospels in which the adult Jesus has a conversation with Mary. He does not address her as "Mother" but as "Woman". In [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") (the language that the Gospel of John was composed in), calling one's mother "Woman" was not disrespectful, and could even be tender.[[63]](#cite_note-68) Accordingly, some versions of the Bible translate it as "Dear woman".[[64]](#cite_note-69)
* In the [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles"), Mary and the [brothers of Jesus](/wiki/Brothers_of_Jesus "Brothers of Jesus") are mentioned in the company of the eleven apostles who are gathered in the [upper room](/wiki/Cenacle "Cenacle") after the [Ascension of Jesus](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus") (Acts 1:14).[[65]](#cite_note-70)

In the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation"), also part of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), the "[woman clothed with the sun](/wiki/Woman_of_the_Apocalypse "Woman of the Apocalypse")" (Revelation 12:1, 12:5–6)[[66]](#cite_note-71) is sometimes identified as Mary.

#### Genealogy

Further information: [Genealogy of Jesus](/wiki/Genealogy_of_Jesus "Genealogy of Jesus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Virgin%27s_first_seven_steps.jpg/250px-Virgin%27s_first_seven_steps.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virgin%27s_first_seven_steps.jpg)

The Virgin's first seven steps, mosaic from [Chora Church](/wiki/Chora_Church "Chora Church"), c. 12th century

The New Testament tells little of Mary's early history. The Gospel of Matthew gives a genealogy for Jesus by his father's paternal line, only identifying Mary as the wife of Joseph. John 19:25[[67]](#cite_note-72) states that Mary had a sister; semantically it is unclear if this sister is the same as [Mary of Clopas](/wiki/Mary_of_Clopas "Mary of Clopas"), or if she is left unnamed. [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome") identifies Mary of Clopas as the sister of Mary, mother of Jesus.[[68]](#cite_note-73) According to the early 2nd century historian [Hegesippus](/wiki/Hegesippus_(chronicler) "Hegesippus (chronicler)"), Mary of Clopas was likely Mary's sister-in-law, understanding Clopas (Cleophas) to have been Joseph's brother.[[69]](#cite_note-74)

According to the writer of Luke, Mary was a relative of [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elisabeth_(biblical_person) "Elisabeth (biblical person)"), wife of the priest [Zechariah](/wiki/Zechariah_(priest) "Zechariah (priest)") of the priestly division of [Abijah](/wiki/Abijah "Abijah"), who was herself part of the [lineage of Aaron](/wiki/Daughter_of_Aaron "Daughter of Aaron") and so of the [Tribe of Levi](/wiki/Tribe_of_Levi "Tribe of Levi").[[70]](#cite_note-75) Some of those who believe that the relationship with Elizabeth was on the maternal side, believe that Mary, like Joseph, was of the royal [Davidic line](/wiki/Davidic_line "Davidic line") and so of the [Tribe of Judah](/wiki/Tribe_of_Judah "Tribe of Judah"), and that the [genealogy of Jesus](/wiki/Genealogy_of_Jesus "Genealogy of Jesus") presented in [Luke 3](/wiki/Luke_3 "Luke 3") from [Nathan](/wiki/Nathan_(son_of_David) "Nathan (son of David)"), is in fact the genealogy of Mary, while the genealogy from [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon") given in [Matthew 1](/wiki/Matthew_1 "Matthew 1") is that of Joseph.[[71]](#cite_note-76)[[72]](#cite_note-77)[[73]](#cite_note-78) (Aaron's wife [Elisheba](/wiki/Elisheba "Elisheba") was of the tribe of Judah, so all their descendants are from both Levi and Judah.)[[74]](#cite_note-79)

#### Annunciation

Main article: [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation")

Mary resided in "her own house"[[75]](#cite_note-80) in [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth") in [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee"), possibly with her parents, and during her betrothal—the first stage of a [Jewish marriage](/wiki/Jewish_view_of_marriage "Jewish view of marriage"). Jewish girls were considered marriageable at the age of twelve years and six months, though the actual age of the bride varied with circumstances. The marriage was preceded by the betrothal, after which the bride legally belonged to the bridegroom, though she did not live with him till about a year later, when the marriage was celebrated.[[76]](#cite_note-81)

The [angel](/wiki/Angel "Angel") Gabriel announced to her that she was to be the mother of the promised [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah "Messiah") by conceiving him through the Holy Spirit, and, after initially expressing incredulity at the announcement, she responded, "I am the handmaid of the Lord. Let it be done unto me according to your word."[[77]](#cite_note-82)[[f]](#cite_note-83) Joseph planned to quietly divorce her, but was told her conception was by the Holy Spirit in a dream by "an angel of the Lord"; the angel told him to not hesitate to take her as his wife, which Joseph did, thereby formally completing the wedding rites.[[78]](#cite_note-84)[[79]](#cite_note-85)

Since the angel Gabriel had told Mary that Elizabeth—having previously been barren—was then miraculously pregnant,[[80]](#cite_note-86) Mary hurried to see Elizabeth, who was living with her husband Zechariah in "the hill country..., [in] a city of Juda". Mary arrived at the house and greeted Elizabeth who called Mary "the mother of my Lord", and Mary spoke the words of praise that later became known as the *[Magnificat](/wiki/Magnificat "Magnificat")* from her first word in the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") version.[[81]](#cite_note-87) After about three months, Mary returned to her own house.[[82]](#cite_note-88)

#### Birth of Jesus

Main article: [Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Cr%C3%A8che_-_bergers.jpg/250px-Cr%C3%A8che_-_bergers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cr%C3%A8che_-_bergers.jpg)

The adoration of the shepherds, a [nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene") in France

According to the [gospel of Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), a decree of the [Roman Emperor](/wiki/Roman_Emperor "Roman Emperor") [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") required that Joseph return to his hometown of [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem") to register for a [Roman census](/wiki/Census_of_Quirinius "Census of Quirinius").[[g]](#cite_note-90) While he was there with Mary, she gave birth to Jesus; but because there was no place for them in the inn, she used a [manger](/wiki/Manger "Manger") as a cradle.[[84]](#cite_note-91): p.14[[85]](#cite_note-92) It is not told how old Mary was at the time of the Nativity,[[86]](#cite_note-Levine-2018-93) but attempts have been made to infer it from the age of a typical Jewish mother of that time. Mary Joan Winn Leith represents the view that Jewish girls typically married soon after the onset of puberty,[[87]](#cite_note-94) while according to [Amram Tropper](/w/index.php?title=Amram_Tropper&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amram Tropper (page does not exist)"), Jewish females generally married later in Palestine and the Western Diaspora than in Babylonia.[[88]](#cite_note-Tropper-2006-95) Some scholars hold the view that among them it typically happened between their mid and late teen years[[89]](#cite_note-96) or late teens and early twenties.[[86]](#cite_note-Levine-2018-93)[[88]](#cite_note-Tropper-2006-95) After eight days, the boy was [circumcised](/wiki/Circumcision_of_Jesus "Circumcision of Jesus") according to Jewish law and named "[Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_(name) "Jesus (name)")" (ישוע, *[Yeshu'a](/wiki/Yeshua_(name) "Yeshua (name)")*), which means "[Yahweh](/wiki/Yahweh "Yahweh") is salvation".[[90]](#cite_note-97)

After Mary continued in the "[blood of her purifying](/wiki/Tumah_and_taharah "Tumah and taharah")" another 33 days, for a total of 40 days, she brought her [burnt offering](/wiki/Burnt_offering_(Judaism) "Burnt offering (Judaism)") and [sin offering](/wiki/Sin_offering "Sin offering") to the [Temple](/wiki/Second_Temple "Second Temple") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") (Luke 2:22),[[91]](#cite_note-98) so the priest could make atonement for her.[[92]](#cite_note-99) They also presented Jesus – "As it is written in the law of the Lord, Every male that openeth the womb shall be called holy to the Lord" (Luke 2:23; Exodus 13:2; 23:12–15; 22:29; 34:19–20; Numbers 3:13; 18:15).[[93]](#cite_note-100) After the prophecies of [Simeon](/wiki/Simeon_(Gospel_of_Luke) "Simeon (Gospel of Luke)") and the prophetess [Anna](/wiki/Anna_(Bible) "Anna (Bible)") in Luke 2:25–38,[[94]](#cite_note-101) the family "returned into Galilee, to their own city Nazareth".[[95]](#cite_note-102)

According to the [gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), [magi](/wiki/Biblical_Magi "Biblical Magi") coming from Eastern regions arrived at Bethlehem where Jesus and his family were living, and worshiped him. Joseph was then warned in a dream that [King Herod](/wiki/Herod_the_Great "Herod the Great") wanted to murder the infant, and the [family](/wiki/Holy_Family "Holy Family") [fled by night to Egypt](/wiki/Flight_into_Egypt "Flight into Egypt") and stayed there for some time. After Herod's death in 4 BC, they returned to Nazareth in Galilee, rather than Bethlehem, because Herod's son [Archelaus](/wiki/Herod_Archelaus "Herod Archelaus") was the ruler of Judaea.[[96]](#cite_note-103)

Mary is involved in the only event in Jesus' adolescent life that is recorded in the New Testament. At the age of 12, Jesus, having become separated from his parents on their return journey from the [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") celebration in Jerusalem, was [found in the Temple](/wiki/Finding_in_the_Temple "Finding in the Temple") among the religious teachers.[[97]](#cite_note-104): p.210[[98]](#cite_note-105)

#### Ministry of Jesus

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/W%C3%BCger_Kreuzigung.jpg/250px-W%C3%BCger_Kreuzigung.jpg)](/wiki/File:W%C3%BCger_Kreuzigung.jpg)

*[Stabat Mater](/wiki/Stabat_Mater_(art) "Stabat Mater (art)")* by [Gabriel Wuger](/wiki/Gabriel_Wuger "Gabriel Wuger"), 1868

Mary was present when, at her suggestion, Jesus worked his first miracle during a [wedding at Cana](/wiki/Wedding_at_Cana "Wedding at Cana") by turning water into wine.[[99]](#cite_note-106) Subsequently, there are events when Mary is mentioned along with the [Jesus' brothers](/wiki/Brothers_of_Jesus "Brothers of Jesus").[[100]](#cite_note-107) According to [Epiphanius](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis"), [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen") and [Eusebius](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Cesarea "Eusebius of Cesarea"), these "brothers" would be sons of Joseph from a previous marriage. This view is still the official position of the Eastern Orthodox churches. Following [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome"), those would be actually Jesus' cousins, children of Mary's sister. This remains the official Roman Catholic position. For [Helvidius](/wiki/Helvidius "Helvidius"), those would be full siblings of Jesus, born to Mary and Joseph after the firstborn Jesus. This has been the most common Protestant position.[[101]](#cite_note-108)[[102]](#cite_note-109)[[103]](#cite_note-110)

The [hagiography](/wiki/Hagiography "Hagiography") of Mary and the [Holy Family](/wiki/Holy_Family "Holy Family") can be contrasted with other material in the Gospels. These references include an incident which can be interpreted as Jesus rejecting his family in the New Testament: "And his mother and his brothers arrived, and standing outside, they sent in a message asking for him ... And looking at those who sat in a circle around him, Jesus said, 'These are my mother and my brothers. Whoever does the will of God is my brother, and sister, and mother'."[[104]](#cite_note-111)[[105]](#cite_note-112)

Mary is also depicted as being present in a [group of women](/wiki/Women_at_the_crucifixion "Women at the crucifixion") at the [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") standing near the [disciple whom Jesus loved](/wiki/Disciple_whom_Jesus_loved "Disciple whom Jesus loved") along with [Mary of Clopas](/wiki/Mary_of_Clopas "Mary of Clopas") and [Mary Magdalene](/wiki/Mary_Magdalene "Mary Magdalene"),[[62]](#cite_note-John_19-67) to which list Matthew 27:56[[106]](#cite_note-113) adds "the mother of the sons of Zebedee", presumably the [Salome](/wiki/Salome_(disciple) "Salome (disciple)") mentioned in Mark 15:40.[[107]](#cite_note-114)

#### After the Ascension of Jesus

In Acts 1:12–26,[[108]](#cite_note-115) especially verse 14, Mary is the only one other than the [eleven apostles](/wiki/Twelve_apostles "Twelve apostles") to be mentioned by name who abode in the [upper room](/wiki/Cenacle "Cenacle"), when they returned from [Mount Olivet](/wiki/Mount_of_Olives "Mount of Olives"). Her presence with the apostles during the [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") is not explicit, although it has been held as a fact by Christian tradition.[[109]](#cite_note-116)

From this time, she disappears from the biblical accounts, although it is held by Catholics that she is again portrayed as the [heavenly woman](/wiki/Woman_of_the_Apocalypse "Woman of the Apocalypse") in the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation").[[110]](#cite_note-117)

Her death is not recorded in the scriptures, but Orthodox tradition, tolerated also by Catholics, has her first dying a natural death, known as the [Dormition of Mary](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Mother_of_God "Dormition of the Mother of God"),[[111]](#cite_note-118) and then, soon after, her body itself also being [assumed](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") (taken bodily) into [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven#In_Roman_Catholicism "Heaven"). Belief in the corporeal assumption of Mary is a [dogma](/wiki/Dogma_in_the_Catholic_Church "Dogma in the Catholic Church") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), in the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") and [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") alike, and is believed as well by the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"),[[112]](#cite_note-119)[[113]](#cite_note-120) the [Oriental Orthodox Church](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), and parts of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") and [Continuing Anglican movement](/wiki/Continuing_Anglican_movement "Continuing Anglican movement").[[114]](#cite_note-121)

Some [apocryphal](/wiki/Apocryphal "Apocryphal") texts add further narrative elements to the story of Mary, although scholars generally consider such texts to be historically mainly unreliable.[[115]](#cite_note-122) According to the [Gospel of James](/wiki/Gospel_of_James "Gospel of James"), Mary was the daughter of [Joachim](/wiki/Joachim "Joachim") and [Anne](/wiki/Saint_Anne "Saint Anne"). Before Mary's conception, Anne had been barren and was far advanced in years. Mary was given to service as a consecrated virgin in the Temple in Jerusalem when she was three years old.[[116]](#cite_note-123) This event is commemorated by some Christian churches as a [liturgical feast](/wiki/Liturgical_feast "Liturgical feast"), the [Presentation of Mary](/wiki/Presentation_of_Mary "Presentation of Mary"), even though its premise conflicts with common understandings of Jewish culture and temple customs.[[117]](#cite_note-124) The Gospel of James also states that Mary was 12–14 years old at the time of her betrothal to Joseph[[118]](#cite_note-125) and that she was 16–17 during her pregnancy, according to most manuscripts.[[119]](#cite_note-126)[[120]](#cite_note-127)[[1]](#cite_note-NewAdvent-1)[[121]](#cite_note-128) According to ancient Jewish custom, Mary could have been betrothed at 12,[[122]](#cite_note-129) but some scholars hold the view that in [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea") betrothal typically happened later.[[86]](#cite_note-Levine-2018-93) The text underlines that the relationship between Mary and Joseph was a non-sexual social arrangement, and that Mary was dedicated to God. It emphasizes holiness of Mary and the virginal nature of her pregnancy.[[123]](#cite_note-130)

Although the canonical gospels contain no account of Mary's life after the resurrection, texts written centuries later provide some embellishment. The origin and authenticity of these details are unknown. The earliest extant biographical writing on Mary is *[Life of the Virgin](/wiki/Life_of_the_Virgin_(Maximus) "Life of the Virgin (Maximus)")*, attributed to the 7th-century saint [Maximus the Confessor](/wiki/Maximus_the_Confessor "Maximus the Confessor"), which portrays her as a key element of the [early Christian Church](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") after the death of Jesus.[[124]](#cite_note-131)[[125]](#cite_note-132)[[126]](#cite_note-133) [Hyppolitus of Thebes](/wiki/Hyppolitus_of_Thebes "Hyppolitus of Thebes"), writing in the 7th or 8th century, says that Mary lived for 11 years after the death of her son, dying in 41 AD.[[127]](#cite_note-134)

### Christian

See also: [Mariology](/wiki/Mariology "Mariology"), [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"), and [Hymns to Mary](/wiki/Hymns_to_Mary "Hymns to Mary")

Christian Marian perspectives include a great deal of diversity. While some Christians such as Catholics and Eastern Orthodox have well established Marian traditions, Protestants at large pay scant attention to [Mariological](/wiki/Mariology "Mariology") themes. Catholics, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Lutherans and Anglicans [venerate](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration") the Virgin Mary. This veneration especially takes the form of [prayer](/wiki/Prayer "Prayer") for intercession with her Son, Jesus Christ. Additionally, it includes composing poems and songs in Mary's honor, painting [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon") or carving statues of her, and [conferring titles on Mary](/wiki/Titles_of_Mary "Titles of Mary") that reflect her position among the saints.[[30]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2003-34)[[31]](#cite_note-Fairbairn-2002-35)[[129]](#cite_note-Flinn-2007-136)[[130]](#cite_note-Schroedel-2006-137)

#### Catholic

Main article: [Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church")

In the Catholic Church, Mary is accorded the title "Blessed" (*beata*, μακάρια, *makaria*) in recognition of her assumption to Heaven and her capacity to intercede on behalf of those who pray to her. There is a difference between the usage of the term "blessed" as pertaining to Mary and its usage as pertaining to a [beatified](/wiki/Beatified "Beatified") person. "Blessed" as a Marian title refers to her exalted state as being the greatest among the saints; for a person who has been declared beatified, on the other hand, "blessed" simply indicates that they may be venerated despite not being [canonized](/wiki/Canonized "Canonized"). Catholic teachings make clear that Mary is not considered divine and prayers to her are not answered by her, but rather by God through her intercession.[[131]](#cite_note-138) The [four Catholic dogmas](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Mariology "Roman Catholic Mariology") regarding Mary are: her status as *Theotokos*, or Mother of God; her perpetual virginity; the Immaculate Conception; and her bodily Assumption into Heaven.[[132]](#cite_note-Fahlbusch-2003-139)[[133]](#cite_note-140)[[134]](#cite_note-141)

The [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_Roman_Catholicism "Veneration of Mary in Roman Catholicism"), the mother of Jesus has a more central role in Roman Catholic teachings and beliefs than in any other major Christian group. Not only do Roman Catholics have more theological doctrines and teachings that relate to Mary, but they have more feasts, prayers, devotional and venerative practices than any other group.[[129]](#cite_note-Flinn-2007-136) The *[Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church")* states: "The Church's devotion to the Blessed Virgin is intrinsic to Christian worship."[[135]](#cite_note-ccc971-142)

For centuries, Catholics have performed acts of [consecration and entrustment to Mary](/wiki/Consecration_and_entrustment_to_Mary "Consecration and entrustment to Mary") at personal, societal and regional levels. These acts may be directed to the Virgin herself, to the [Immaculate Heart of Mary](/wiki/Immaculate_Heart_of_Mary "Immaculate Heart of Mary") and to the [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception"). In Catholic teachings, consecration to Mary does not diminish or substitute the love of God, but enhances it, for all consecration is ultimately made to God.[[136]](#cite_note-143)[[137]](#cite_note-144)

Following the growth of Marian devotions in the 16th century, Catholic saints wrote books such as [*Glories of Mary*](/wiki/The_Glories_of_Mary "The Glories of Mary") and *[True Devotion to Mary](/wiki/True_Devotion_to_Mary "True Devotion to Mary")* that emphasized Marian veneration and taught that "the path to Jesus is through Mary".[[138]](#cite_note-145) Marian devotions are at times linked to [Christocentric](/wiki/Christology "Christology") devotions (such as the [Alliance of the Hearts of Jesus and Mary](/wiki/Alliance_of_the_Hearts_of_Jesus_and_Mary "Alliance of the Hearts of Jesus and Mary")).[[139]](#cite_note-146)

Major Marian devotions include: [Seven Sorrows of Mary](/wiki/Seven_Sorrows_of_Mary "Seven Sorrows of Mary"), [Rosary and scapular](/wiki/Rosary_and_scapular "Rosary and scapular"), [Miraculous Medal](/wiki/Miraculous_Medal "Miraculous Medal") and [Reparations to Mary](/wiki/Acts_of_Reparation_to_the_Virgin_Mary "Acts of Reparation to the Virgin Mary").[[140]](#cite_note-147)[[141]](#cite_note-148) The months of May and October are traditionally "Marian months" for Roman Catholics; the daily [rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary") is encouraged in October and in [May Marian devotions](/wiki/May_devotions_to_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "May devotions to the Blessed Virgin Mary") take place in many regions.[[142]](#cite_note-149)[[143]](#cite_note-150)[[144]](#cite_note-151) Popes have issued a number of [Marian encyclicals and Apostolic Letters](/wiki/Marian_papal_encyclicals_and_Apostolic_Letters "Marian papal encyclicals and Apostolic Letters") to encourage devotions to and the veneration of the Virgin Mary.

Catholics place high emphasis on Mary's roles as protector and intercessor and the [*Catechism*](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church") refers to Mary as "honored with the title 'Mother of God', to whose protection the faithful fly in all their dangers and needs".[[135]](#cite_note-ccc971-142)[[145]](#cite_note-152)[[146]](#cite_note-153)[[147]](#cite_note-154)[[148]](#cite_note-155) Key Marian prayers include: *[Ave Maria](/wiki/Hail_Mary "Hail Mary")*, *[Alma Redemptoris Mater](/wiki/Alma_Redemptoris_Mater "Alma Redemptoris Mater")*, *[Sub tuum praesidium](/wiki/Sub_tuum_praesidium "Sub tuum praesidium")*, *[Ave maris stella](/wiki/Ave_maris_stella "Ave maris stella")*, *[Regina caeli](/wiki/Regina_caeli "Regina caeli")*, *[Ave Regina caelorum](/wiki/Ave_Regina_caelorum "Ave Regina caelorum")* and the *[Magnificat](/wiki/Magnificat "Magnificat")*.[[149]](#cite_note-156)

Mary's participation in the processes of [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_(Christianity) "Salvation (Christianity)") and redemption has also been emphasized in the Catholic tradition, but they are not doctrines.[[150]](#cite_note-157)[[151]](#cite_note-158)[[152]](#cite_note-159)[[153]](#cite_note-160) [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II")'s 1987 encyclical *[Redemptoris Mater](/wiki/Redemptoris_Mater "Redemptoris Mater")* began with the sentence: "The Mother of the Redeemer has a precise place in the plan of salvation."[[154]](#cite_note-161)

In the 20th century, both popes John Paul II and [Benedict XVI](/wiki/Benedict_XVI "Benedict XVI") emphasized the Marian focus of the Catholic Church. Cardinal [Joseph Ratzinger](/wiki/Joseph_Ratzinger "Joseph Ratzinger") (later Pope Benedict XVI) suggested a redirection of the whole church towards the program of Pope John Paul II in order to ensure an authentic approach to [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology") via a return to the "whole truth about Mary,"[[155]](#cite_note-Miravalle-Burke-2008-162) writing:

> "It is necessary to go back to Mary if we want to return to that 'truth about Jesus Christ,' 'truth about the Church' and 'truth about man.'"[[155]](#cite_note-Miravalle-Burke-2008-162)

There is significant diversity in the Marian doctrines attributed to her primarily by the Catholic Church. The key Marian doctrines held primarily in Catholicism can be briefly outlined as follows:

* [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception"): Mary was conceived without [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin").
* [Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God"): Mary, as the mother of Jesus, is the *Theotokos* (God-bearer), or Mother of God.
* [Virgin birth of Jesus](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus"): Mary conceived Jesus by action of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_(Christianity) "Holy Spirit (Christianity)") while remaining a virgin.
* [Perpetual Virginity](/wiki/Perpetual_Virginity "Perpetual Virginity"): Mary remained a virgin all her life, even after the act of giving birth to Jesus.
* [Dormition](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Mother_of_God "Dormition of the Mother of God"): commemorates Mary's "falling asleep" or natural death shortly before her Assumption. Dormition is part of accepted [Eastern Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") theology, but not part of [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_(term) "Roman Catholic (term)") doctrine.[[156]](#cite_note-163)
* [Assumption](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary"): Mary was taken [bodily into heaven](/wiki/Entering_heaven_alive "Entering heaven alive") either at, or before, her death.

The acceptance of these Marian doctrines by Roman Catholics and other Christians can be summarized as follows:[[21]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2004-24)[[157]](#cite_note-164)[[158]](#cite_note-165)

| Doctrine | Church action | Accepted by |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Virgin birth of Jesus](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus") | [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea"), 325 | Catholics, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Assyrians, Lutherans, Anglicans, Baptists and other Protestants |
| [Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God") | [First Council of Ephesus](/wiki/First_Council_of_Ephesus "First Council of Ephesus"), 431 | Catholics, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Lutherans, Anglicans, Methodists, some Evangelicals.[[159]](#cite_note-166) |
| [Perpetual Virginity](/wiki/Perpetual_Virginity "Perpetual Virginity") | [Second Ecumenical Council of Constantinople](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Constantinople "Second Council of Constantinople"), 553 | Catholics, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Assyrians, many Lutherans, some Anglicans[[23]](#cite_note-Smith-2010-26)[[24]](#cite_note-Anderson-1992-27)[[160]](#cite_note-167) |
| [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception") | *[Ineffabilis Deus](/wiki/Ineffabilis_Deus "Ineffabilis Deus")* encyclical  [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX"), 1854 | Catholics, some Oriental Orthodox,[[161]](#cite_note-168)[[162]](#cite_note-169) some Anglicans, some Lutherans (early Martin Luther) |
| [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") | *[Munificentissimus Deus](/wiki/Munificentissimus_Deus "Munificentissimus Deus")* encyclical  [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII"), 1950 | Catholics, Eastern and Oriental Orthodox (only following her natural death), some Lutherans, some Anglicans |

The title "Mother of God" (*Theotokos*) for Mary was confirmed by the [First Council of Ephesus](/wiki/First_Council_of_Ephesus "First Council of Ephesus"), held at the [Church of Mary](/wiki/Church_of_Mary "Church of Mary") in 431. The Council decreed that Mary is the Mother of God because her son Jesus is one person who is both God and man, divine and human.[[34]](#cite_note-ccel.org-38) This doctrine is widely accepted by Christians in general, and the term "Mother of God" had already been used within the oldest known prayer to Mary, the *[Sub tuum praesidium](/wiki/Sub_tuum_praesidium "Sub tuum praesidium")*, which dates to around 250 AD.[[163]](#cite_note-170)[[23]](#cite_note-Smith-2010-26)[[24]](#cite_note-Anderson-1992-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e9/Saint_Mary_of_Lukawiec-Saint_Mary_of_Tartakow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Mary_of_Lukawiec-Saint_Mary_of_Tartakow.jpg)

Miraculous Icon of Our Lady of Tartaków in Blessed Virgin Mary Church in Łukawiec

The [Virgin birth of Jesus](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus") was an almost universally held belief among Christians from the 2nd until the 19th century.[[164]](#cite_note-171) It is included in the two most widely used Christian [creeds](/wiki/Creed "Creed"), which state that Jesus "was incarnate of the Holy Spirit and the Virgin Mary" (the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed"), in what is now its familiar form)[[165]](#cite_note-172) and the [Apostles' Creed](/wiki/Apostles%27_Creed "Apostles' Creed"). The [Gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") describes Mary as a virgin who fulfilled the prophecy of Isaiah 7:14,[[166]](#cite_note-173) The authors of the Gospels of Matthew and Luke consider Jesus' conception not the result of intercourse, and assert that Mary had "no relations with man" before Jesus' birth.[[167]](#cite_note-174) This alludes to the belief that Mary conceived Jesus through the action of God the Holy Spirit, and not through [intercourse](/wiki/Human_reproduction#Copulation "Human reproduction") with Joseph or anyone else.[[168]](#cite_note-Miravalle-1993-175)

The doctrines of the Assumption or Dormition of Mary relate to her death and bodily assumption to heaven. Roman Catholic Church has dogmatically defined the doctrine of the Assumption, which was done in 1950 by [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") in *[Munificentissimus Deus](/wiki/Munificentissimus_Deus "Munificentissimus Deus")*. Whether Mary died or not is not defined dogmatically, however, although a reference to the death of Mary is made in *Munificentissimus Deus*. In the Eastern Orthodox Church, the Assumption of the Virgin Mary is believed, and celebrated with her [Dormition](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Mother_of_God "Dormition of the Mother of God"), where they believe she died.

Catholics believe in the [Immaculate Conception of Mary](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception_of_Mary "Immaculate Conception of Mary"), as proclaimed *[ex cathedra](/wiki/Ex_cathedra "Ex cathedra")* by Pope [Pius IX](/wiki/Pius_IX "Pius IX") in 1854, namely that she was filled with grace from the very moment of her conception in her mother's womb and preserved from the stain of [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin"). The [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") has a liturgical [feast by that name](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Feast of the Immaculate Conception"), kept on 8 December.[[169]](#cite_note-176) Orthodox Christians reject the Immaculate Conception dogma principally because their understanding of ancestral sin (the Greek term corresponding to the Latin "original sin") differs from the [Augustinian](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") interpretation and that of the Catholic Church.[[170]](#cite_note-177)

The Perpetual Virginity of Mary asserts Mary's real and perpetual [virginity](/wiki/Virginity "Virginity") even in the act of giving birth to the Son of God made Man. The term Ever-Virgin (Greek ἀειπάρθενος) is applied in this case, stating that Mary remained a virgin for the remainder of her life, making Jesus her biological and only son, whose [conception](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)") and [birth](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") are held to be miraculous.[[132]](#cite_note-Fahlbusch-2003-139)[[168]](#cite_note-Miravalle-1993-175)[[171]](#cite_note-178) The Orthodox Churches hold the position articulated in the [Protoevangelium of James](/wiki/Gospel_of_James "Gospel of James") that Jesus' brothers and sisters were Joseph's children from a marriage prior to that of Mary, which had left him widowed. Roman Catholic teaching follows the Latin father [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome") in considering them Jesus' cousins.

#### Eastern Orthodox

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Santa_Sofia_-_Mosaic_de_Joan_II_Comn%C3%A8_i_la_seva_esposa%2C_Irene.JPG/250px-Santa_Sofia_-_Mosaic_de_Joan_II_Comn%C3%A8_i_la_seva_esposa%2C_Irene.JPG)](/wiki/File:Santa_Sofia_-_Mosaic_de_Joan_II_Comn%C3%A8_i_la_seva_esposa,_Irene.JPG)

A [mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic "Mosaic") from the [Hagia Sophia](/wiki/Hagia_Sophia "Hagia Sophia") of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") (modern Istanbul), depicting Mary with Jesus, flanked by [John II Komnenos](/wiki/John_II_Komnenos "John II Komnenos") (left) and his wife [Irene of Hungary](/wiki/Irene_of_Hungary "Irene of Hungary") (right), c. 1118 AD

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/%D0%9C%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%87%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0_1405_%D0%9F%D1%83%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%8C.jpg/250px-%D0%9C%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%87%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0_1405_%D0%9F%D1%83%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%8C.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9C%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%87%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B8%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0_1405_%D0%9F%D1%83%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%8C.jpg)

15th century [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of the *Theotokos* ("God-bearer")

[Eastern Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") includes a large number of traditions regarding the Ever-Virgin Mary, the *Theotokos*.[[172]](#cite_note-179) The Orthodox believe that she was and remained a virgin before and after Christ's birth.[[31]](#cite_note-Fairbairn-2002-35) The *[Theotokia](/wiki/Theotokia "Theotokia")* ([hymns to the Theotokos](/wiki/Hymns_to_Mary "Hymns to Mary")) are an essential part of the [Divine Services](/wiki/Divine_Services "Divine Services") in the [Eastern Church](/wiki/Eastern_Church "Eastern Church") and their positioning within the liturgical sequence effectively places the *Theotokos* in the most prominent place after Christ.[[173]](#cite_note-Dragas-2005-180) Within the Orthodox tradition, the order of the saints begins with: the *Theotokos*, Angels, Prophets, Apostles, Fathers and Martyrs, giving the Virgin Mary precedence over the angels. She is also proclaimed as the "Lady of the Angels".[[173]](#cite_note-Dragas-2005-180)

The views of the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") still play an important role in the shaping of Orthodox Marian perspective. However, the Orthodox views on Mary are mostly [doxological](/wiki/Doxology "Doxology"), rather than academic: they are expressed in hymns, praise, liturgical poetry, and the veneration of icons. One of the most loved Orthodox [Akathists](/wiki/Akathist "Akathist") ([standing hymns](/wiki/Akathist_to_the_Theotokos "Akathist to the Theotokos")) is devoted to Mary and it is often simply called the *[Akathist Hymn](/wiki/Akathist_to_the_Theotokos "Akathist to the Theotokos")*.[[174]](#cite_note-181) Five of the twelve [Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts "Great Feasts") in Orthodoxy are dedicated to Mary.[[31]](#cite_note-Fairbairn-2002-35) The [Sunday of Orthodoxy](/wiki/Sunday_of_Orthodoxy "Sunday of Orthodoxy") directly links the Virgin Mary's identity as Mother of God with icon veneration.[[175]](#cite_note-182)
A number of Orthodox feasts are connected with the miraculous icons of the *Theotokos*.[[173]](#cite_note-Dragas-2005-180)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Virgin_of_the_Passion_by_E.Tzanfournaris.jpg/250px-Virgin_of_the_Passion_by_E.Tzanfournaris.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virgin_of_the_Passion_by_E.Tzanfournaris.jpg)

*(Panagía tou Páthous) [Virgin of the Passion](/wiki/Virgin_of_the_Passion "Virgin of the Passion")* by [Emmanuel Tzanfournaris](/wiki/Emmanuel_Tzanfournaris "Emmanuel Tzanfournaris"), early 1600s

The Orthodox view Mary as "superior to all created beings", although not divine.[[176]](#cite_note-Bulgakov-1997-183) As such, the designation of Saint to Mary as Saint Mary is not appropriate.[[177]](#cite_note-184) The Orthodox does not venerate Mary as conceived immaculate. [Gregory of Nazianzus](/wiki/Gregory_of_Nazianzus "Gregory of Nazianzus"), [Archbishop of Constantinople](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Constantinople "Archbishop of Constantinople") in the 4th century AD, speaking on the Nativity of Jesus Christ argues that "Conceived by the Virgin, who first in body and soul was purified by the Holy Ghost, He came forth as God with that which He had assumed, One Person in two Natures, Flesh and Spirit, of which the latter defined the former."[[178]](#cite_note-185) The Orthodox celebrate the [Dormition of the Theotokos](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Theotokos "Dormition of the Theotokos"), rather than Assumption.[[31]](#cite_note-Fairbairn-2002-35)

The [Protoevangelium of James](/wiki/Protoevangelium_of_James "Protoevangelium of James"), an [extra-canonical](/wiki/Biblical_canon "Biblical canon") book, has been the source of many Orthodox beliefs on Mary. The account of Mary's life presented includes her consecration as a virgin at the temple at age three. The [high priest](/wiki/Kohen "Kohen") Zachariah blessed Mary and informed her that God had magnified her name among many generations. Zachariah placed Mary on the third step of the altar, whereby God gave her grace. While in the temple, Mary was miraculously fed by an angel, until she was 12 years old. At that point, an angel told Zachariah to betroth Mary to a widower in Israel, who would be indicated. This story provides the theme of many hymns for the Feast of [Presentation of Mary](/wiki/Presentation_of_Mary "Presentation of Mary"), and icons of the feast depict the story.[[179]](#cite_note-186) The Orthodox believe that Mary was instrumental in the growth of Christianity during the life of Jesus, and after his Crucifixion, and Orthodox theologian [Sergei Bulgakov](/wiki/Sergei_Bulgakov "Sergei Bulgakov") has written: "The Virgin Mary is the centre, invisible, but real, of the Apostolic Church."

Theologians from the Orthodox tradition have made prominent contributions to the development of Marian thought and devotion.
[John Damascene](/wiki/John_Damascene "John Damascene") (c. 650 – c. 750) was one of the greatest Orthodox theologians. Among other Marian writings, he proclaimed the essential nature of Mary's heavenly Assumption or Dormition and her meditative role.

> It was necessary that the body of the one who preserved her virginity intact in giving birth should also be kept incorrupt after death. It was necessary that she, who carried the Creator in her womb when he was a baby, should dwell among the tabernacles of heaven.[[180]](#cite_note-187)

> From her we have harvested the grape of life; from her we have cultivated the seed of immortality. For our sake she became Mediatrix of all blessings; in her God became man, and man became God.[[181]](#cite_note-188)

More recently, [Sergei Bulgakov](/wiki/Sergei_Bulgakov "Sergei Bulgakov") expressed the Orthodox sentiments towards Mary as follows:[[176]](#cite_note-Bulgakov-1997-183)

> Mary is not merely the instrument, but the direct positive condition of the Incarnation, its human aspect. Christ could not have been incarnate by some mechanical process, violating human nature. It was necessary for that nature itself to say for itself, by the mouth of the most pure human being: "Behold the handmaid of the Lord, be it unto me according to Thy word."

#### Protestant

Further information: [Protestant views on Mary](/wiki/Protestant_views_on_Mary "Protestant views on Mary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/MaryattheTomb.jpg/250px-MaryattheTomb.jpg)](/wiki/File:MaryattheTomb.jpg)

Stained glass window of [Jesus leaving his mother](/wiki/Christ_taking_leave_of_his_Mother "Christ taking leave of his Mother"), in a [Lutheran church](/wiki/St._Matthew%27s_German_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church "St. Matthew's German Evangelical Lutheran Church") in South Carolina

Protestants in general reject the veneration and invocation of the Saints.[[21]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2004-24): 1174 They share the belief that Mary is the mother of Jesus and "blessed among women" (Luke 1:42)[[182]](#cite_note-189) but they generally do not agree that Mary is to be venerated. She is considered to be an outstanding example of a life dedicated to God.[[183]](#cite_note-190) As such, they tend not to accept certain church doctrines such as her being preserved from sin.[[184]](#cite_note-191) Theologian [Karl Barth](/wiki/Karl_Barth "Karl Barth") wrote that "the heresy of the Catholic Church is its [Mariology](/wiki/Mariology "Mariology")".[[185]](#cite_note-192)

Early Protestantism had high regard for Mary. [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") wrote that: "Mary is full of grace, proclaimed to be entirely without sin. God's grace fills her with everything good and makes her devoid of all evil."[[186]](#cite_note-193) [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") remarked, "It cannot be denied that God in choosing and destining Mary to be the Mother of his Son, granted her the highest honor."[[h]](#cite_note-196) However, Calvin firmly rejected the notion that Mary can intercede between Christ and man.[[189]](#cite_note-197)

Although Calvin and [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli") honored Mary as the Mother of Christ in the 16th century, they did so less than Martin Luther.[[190]](#cite_note-198) Thus the idea of respect and high honor for Mary was not rejected by the first Protestants; however, they came to criticize the Roman Catholics for venerating Mary. Following the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") in the 16th century, as Marian veneration became associated with Catholics, Protestant interest in Mary decreased. During the Age of the Enlightenment, any residual interest in Mary within Protestant churches almost disappeared, although Anglicans and Lutherans continued to honor her.[[21]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2004-24)

In the 20th century, some Protestants reacted in opposition to the Catholic dogma of the [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary").[[191]](#cite_note-199) The tone of the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") began to mend the ecumenical differences, and Protestants began to show interest in Marian themes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1997 and 1998, ecumenical dialogues between Catholics and Protestants took place, but, to date, the majority of Protestants disagree with Marian veneration and some view it as a challenge to the [authority of Scripture](/wiki/Sola_Scriptura "Sola Scriptura").[[21]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2004-24)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

##### Lutheran

Main article: [Lutheran Mariology](/wiki/Lutheran_Mariology "Lutheran Mariology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Gettorf_Altar.jpg/250px-Gettorf_Altar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gettorf_Altar.jpg)

Mary with an inscription referencing Luke 1:46–47 in St. Jürgen (Lutheran) church in [Gettorf](/wiki/Gettorf "Gettorf") (Schleswig-Holstein)

[Evangelical-Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") regards her as the *Blessed Virgin Mary*, with this codified in the Formula of Concord.[[24]](#cite_note-Anderson-1992-27)[[192]](#cite_note-200) The Evangelical-Lutheran Churches celebrate several Marian feast days, including the [Feast of the Annunciation of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Annunciation "Feast of the Annunciation"), [Feast of the Purification of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas"), and the [Visitation of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Visitation_(Christianity) "Visitation (Christianity)").[[22]](#cite_note-Wengert-2017-25) The [Formula of Concord](/wiki/Formula_of_Concord "Formula of Concord") and the [Smalcald Articles](/wiki/Smalcald_Articles "Smalcald Articles"), upheld by the Lutheran Churches, teach the [virgin birth](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus"), *[Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos")* and the [perpetual virginity of Mary](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary").[[23]](#cite_note-Smith-2010-26)[[24]](#cite_note-Anderson-1992-27) The [Apology of the Augsburg Confession](/wiki/Apology_of_the_Augsburg_Confession "Apology of the Augsburg Confession") teaches that "blessed Mary prays for the Church".[[193]](#cite_note-201)

Theologians agree that Martin Luther adhered to the Marian decrees of the [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council") and dogmas of the church. He held fast to the belief that Mary was a perpetual virgin and Mother of God.[[194]](#cite_note-202)[[195]](#cite_note-203) Special attention is given to the assertion that Luther, some 300 years before the dogmatization of the [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception") by [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") in 1854, was a firm adherent of that view.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Others maintain that Luther in later years changed his position on the Immaculate Conception, which, at that time was undefined in the church, maintaining however the [sinlessness of Mary throughout her life](/wiki/Sinlessness_of_Mary "Sinlessness of Mary").[[196]](#cite_note-204)[[197]](#cite_note-205) For Luther, early in his life, the Assumption of Mary was an understood fact, although he later stated that the Bible did not say anything about it and stopped celebrating its feast. Important to him was the belief that Mary and the saints do live on after death.[[198]](#cite_note-206)[[199]](#cite_note-207)[[200]](#cite_note-208) "Throughout his career as a priest-professor-reformer, Luther preached, taught, and argued about the veneration of Mary with a verbosity that ranged from childlike piety to sophisticated polemics. His views are intimately linked to his Christocentric theology and its consequences for liturgy and piety."[[201]](#cite_note-209) Luther, while revering Mary, came to criticize the "Papists" for blurring the line between high admiration of the grace of God wherever it is seen in a human being, and religious service given to another creature. He considered the Roman Catholic practice of celebrating [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint")' days and making intercessory requests addressed especially to Mary and other departed saints to be [idolatry](/wiki/Idolatry "Idolatry").[[202]](#cite_note-210)[[203]](#cite_note-211) His final thoughts on Marian devotion and veneration are preserved in a sermon preached at Wittenberg only a month before his death:

> Therefore, when we preach faith, that we should worship nothing but God alone, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, as we say in the Creed: 'I believe in God the Father almighty and in Jesus Christ,' then we are remaining in the temple at Jerusalem. Again,'This is my beloved Son; listen to him.' 'You will find him in a manger'. He alone does it. But reason says the opposite:
> What, us? Are we to worship only Christ? Indeed, shouldn't we also honor the holy mother of Christ? She is the woman who bruised the head of the serpent. Hear us, Mary, for thy Son so honors thee that he can refuse thee nothing. Here [Bernard](/wiki/Bernard_of_Clairvaux "Bernard of Clairvaux") went too far in his *Homilies on the Gospel: Missus est Angelus*.[[204]](#cite_note-212) God has commanded that we should honor the parents; therefore I will call upon Mary. She will intercede for me with the Son, and the Son with the Father, who will listen to the Son. So you have the picture of God as angry and Christ as judge; Mary shows to Christ her breast and Christ shows his wounds to the wrathful Father. That's the kind of thing this comely bride, the wisdom of reason cooks up: Mary is the mother of Christ, surely Christ will listen to her; Christ is a stern judge, therefore I will call upon St. George and St. Christopher. No, we have been by God's command baptized in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, just as the Jews were circumcised.[[205]](#cite_note-213)[[206]](#cite_note-214)

Certain Lutheran churches such as the [Anglo-Lutheran Catholic Church](/wiki/Anglo-Lutheran_Catholic_Church "Anglo-Lutheran Catholic Church") venerate Mary and the saints in the same manner that Roman Catholics do, and hold all Marian dogmas as part of their faith.[[207]](#cite_note-215)

##### Anglican

Main article: [Anglican Marian theology](/wiki/Anglican_Marian_theology "Anglican Marian theology")

The various churches that form the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") and the [Continuing Anglican](/wiki/Continuing_Anglican "Continuing Anglican") movement have different views on Marian doctrines and venerative practices given that there is no single church with universal authority within the Communion and that the mother church (the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")) understands itself to be both "Catholic" and "[Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity")".[[208]](#cite_note-216) The Anglican Communion includes segments which still retain some veneration of Mary.[[130]](#cite_note-Schroedel-2006-137)

Mary's special position within God's purpose of salvation as "God-bearer" is recognized in a number of ways by some Anglican Christians.[[209]](#cite_note-Braaten-2004-217) All the member churches of the Anglican Communion affirm in the historic creeds that Jesus was born of the Virgin Mary, and celebrates the feast days of the [Presentation of Christ in the Temple](/wiki/Presentation_of_Christ_in_the_Temple "Presentation of Christ in the Temple"). This feast is called in older [prayer books](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer") the [Purification of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Purification_of_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Purification of the Blessed Virgin Mary") on 2 February. The [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") of our Lord to the Blessed Virgin on 25 March was from before the time of [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") until the 18th century New Year's Day in England. The Annunciation is called the "Annunciation of our Lady" in the 1662 [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer"). Anglicans also celebrate in the [Visitation of the Blessed Virgin](/wiki/Visitation_(Christian) "Visitation (Christian)") on 31 May, though in some provinces the traditional date of 2 July is kept. The feast of the St. Mary the Virgin is observed on the traditional day of the Assumption, 15 August. The [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Nativity of the Blessed Virgin Mary") of the Blessed Virgin is kept on 8 September.[[130]](#cite_note-Schroedel-2006-137)

The Conception of the Blessed Virgin Mary is kept in the 1662 Book of Common Prayer, on 8 December. In certain Anglican parishes of [Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") churchmanship, this feast is called the Immaculate Conception. Again, the Assumption of Mary is believed in by most Anglo-Catholics, but is considered a [pious](/wiki/Piety "Piety") opinion by moderate Anglicans. [Reformed Anglicans](/wiki/Reformed_Anglican "Reformed Anglican") reject the celebration of these feasts.[[130]](#cite_note-Schroedel-2006-137)

Prayers and venerative practices vary greatly. For instance, as of the 19th century, following the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement"), [Anglo-Catholics](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") frequently pray the [Rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary"), the *[Angelus](/wiki/Angelus "Angelus")*, *[Regina caeli](/wiki/Regina_caeli "Regina caeli")*, and other litanies and anthems of Mary reminiscent of Catholic practices.[[210]](#cite_note-218) Conversely, [low church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") Anglicans rarely invoke the Blessed Virgin except in certain hymns, such as the second stanza of *[Ye Watchers and Ye Holy Ones](/wiki/Ye_Watchers_and_Ye_Holy_Ones "Ye Watchers and Ye Holy Ones")*.[[209]](#cite_note-Braaten-2004-217)[[211]](#cite_note-219)

The [Anglican Society of Mary](/wiki/Society_of_Mary_(Anglican) "Society of Mary (Anglican)") was formed in 1931 and maintains chapters in many countries. The purpose of the society is to foster devotion to Mary among Anglicans.[[130]](#cite_note-Schroedel-2006-137)[[212]](#cite_note-220) [High church](/wiki/High_church "High church") Anglicans espouse doctrines that are closer to Roman Catholics, and retain veneration for Mary, such as [Anglican pilgrimages](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage") to [Our Lady of Lourdes](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Lourdes "Our Lady of Lourdes"), which have taken place since 1963, and [pilgrimages](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage") to [Our Lady of Walsingham](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Walsingham "Our Lady of Walsingham"), which have taken place for hundreds of years.[[213]](#cite_note-221)

Historically, there has been enough common ground between Roman Catholics and Anglicans on Marian issues that in 2005, a joint statement called *Mary: grace and hope in Christ* was produced through ecumenical meetings of Anglicans and Roman Catholic theologians. This document, informally known as the "Seattle Statement", is not formally endorsed by either the Catholic Church or the Anglican Communion, but is viewed by its authors as the beginning of a joint understanding of Mary.[[130]](#cite_note-Schroedel-2006-137)[[214]](#cite_note-222)

##### Methodist

Further information: [Saints in Methodism § Virgin Mary](/wiki/Saints_in_Methodism#Virgin_Mary "Saints in Methodism")

Methodists do not have any additional teachings on the Virgin Mary except from what is mentioned in Scripture and the ecumenical Creeds. As such, Methodists generally accept the doctrine of the virgin birth, but reject the doctrine of the Immaculate Conception.[[215]](#cite_note-223) [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley"), the principal founder of the Methodist movement within the Church of England, believed that Mary "continued a [pure and unspotted virgin](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary")", thus upholding the doctrine of the perpetual virginity of Mary.[[216]](#cite_note-224)[[217]](#cite_note-225) Contemporary Methodism holds that Mary was a virgin before, during, and immediately after the birth of Christ.[[218]](#cite_note-226)[[219]](#cite_note-227) In addition, some Methodists also hold the doctrine of the [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") as a pious opinion.[[220]](#cite_note-228)

#### Nontrinitarian

[Nontrinitarians](/wiki/Nontrinitarians "Nontrinitarians"), such as [Unitarians](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism"), [Christadelphians](/wiki/Christadelphians "Christadelphians"), [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), and [Latter Day Saints](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement")[[221]](#cite_note-229) also acknowledge Mary as the biological mother of Jesus Christ, but most reject any immaculate conception and do not recognize Marian titles such as "Mother of God". The [Latter Day Saint movement's view](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_views_on_Mary "Latter Day Saint views on Mary") affirms the virgin birth of Jesus[[222]](#cite_note-230) and Christ's divinity, but only as a separate being than [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"). The [Book of Mormon](/wiki/Book_of_Mormon "Book of Mormon") refers to Mary by name in prophecies and describes her as "most beautiful and fair above all other virgins"[[223]](#cite_note-231) and as a "precious and chosen vessel."[[224]](#cite_note-232)[[225]](#cite_note-233)

In nontrinitarian groups that are also [Christian mortalists](/wiki/Christian_mortalism "Christian mortalism"), Mary is not seen as an intercessor between humankind and Jesus, whom mortalists would consider "asleep", awaiting resurrection.[[226]](#cite_note-234)

### Jewish

The issue of the parentage of [Jesus in the Talmud](/wiki/Jesus_in_the_Talmud "Jesus in the Talmud") also affects Jewish views of Mary. However, the Talmud does not mention Mary by name, and is considerate rather than only polemic.[[227]](#cite_note-235)[[228]](#cite_note-236) The story about [Panthera](/wiki/Panthera_(Jesus%27s_father) "Panthera (Jesus's father)") is also found in the *[Toledot Yeshu](/wiki/Toledot_Yeshu "Toledot Yeshu")*, the literary origins of which can not be traced with any certainty, and given that it is unlikely to go before the 4th century, the time is too late to include authentic remembrances of Jesus.[[229]](#cite_note-237) *The Blackwell Companion to Jesus* states that the *Toledot Yeshu* has no historical facts and was perhaps created as a tool for warding off conversions to Christianity.[[230]](#cite_note-238) The tales from the *Toledot Yeshu* did impart a negative picture of Mary to ordinary Jewish readers.[[231]](#cite_note-Remensnyder-2014-239) The circulation of the *Toledot Yeshu* was widespread among European and Middle Eastern Jewish communities since the 9th century.[[232]](#cite_note-240) The name Panthera may be a distortion of the term *parthenos* ("virgin") and [Raymond E. Brown](/wiki/Raymond_E._Brown "Raymond E. Brown") considers the story of Panthera a fanciful explanation of the birth of Jesus that includes very little historical evidence.[[233]](#cite_note-241) [Robert Van Voorst](/wiki/Robert_Van_Voorst "Robert Van Voorst") states that because *Toledot Yeshu* is a medieval document with its lack of a fixed form and orientation towards a popular audience, it is "most unlikely" to have reliable historical information.[[234]](#cite_note-242) Stacks of the copies of the Talmud were burnt upon a court order after the [1240 Disputation](/wiki/Disputation_of_Paris "Disputation of Paris") for allegedly containing material defaming the character of Mary.[[231]](#cite_note-Remensnyder-2014-239)

### Islamic

Main article: [Mary in Islam](/wiki/Mary_in_Islam "Mary in Islam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Maryam_and_Isa.jpg/250px-Maryam_and_Isa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maryam_and_Isa.jpg)

[Persian miniature](/wiki/Persian_miniature "Persian miniature") of Mary and Jesus

The Virgin Mary holds a singularly exalted place in [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), and she is considered by the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") to have been the greatest woman in the history of humankind. The Islamic scripture recounts the Divine Promise given to Mary as being: ""O Mary! Surely Allah has selected you, purified you, and chosen you over all women of the world" ([3:42](https://quran.com/3?startingVerse=42)).

Mary is often referred to by Muslims by the honorific title *Sayedetina* ("Our Lady"). She is mentioned in the Quran as the daughter of Imran.[[235]](#cite_note-Glasse-2003-243)

Moreover, Mary is the only woman named in the Quran and she is mentioned or referred to in the scripture a total of 50 times.[[i]](#cite_note-244) Mary holds a singularly distinguished and honored position among [women in the Quran](/wiki/Female_figures_in_the_Quran "Female figures in the Quran"). A *[sura](/wiki/Sura "Sura")* (chapter) in the Quran is titled "[Maryam](/wiki/Maryam_(sura) "Maryam (sura)")" (Mary), the only *sura* in the Quran named after a woman, in which the story of Mary (Maryam) and Jesus (Isa) is recounted according to the view of [Jesus in Islam](/wiki/Jesus_in_Islam "Jesus in Islam").[[13]](#cite_note-Jestice-2004-15)

#### Birth

In a narration of [hadith](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith") from Imam [Ja'far al-Sadiq](/wiki/Ja%27far_al-Sadiq "Ja'far al-Sadiq"), he mentions that [Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah") revealed to [Imran](/wiki/Joachim#In_Islamic_tradition "Joachim"), "I will grant you a boy, blessed, one who will cure the blind and the leper and one who will raise the dead by My permission. And I will send him as an apostle to the Children of Israel." Then Imran related the story to his wife, [Hannah](/wiki/Anne_in_Islam "Anne in Islam"), the mother of Mary. When she became pregnant, she conceived it was a boy, but when she gave birth to a girl, she stated "Oh my Lord! Verily I have delivered a female, and the male is not like the female, for a girl will not be a prophet," to which [Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah") replies in the Quran, "Allah knows better what has been delivered" (3:36). When Allah bestowed Jesus to Mary, he fulfilled his promise to Imran.[[236]](#cite_note-245)

#### Motherhood

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Maryamat_the_palm_tree_with_baby_Isa.jpg/250px-Maryamat_the_palm_tree_with_baby_Isa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maryamat_the_palm_tree_with_baby_Isa.jpg)

Islamic [miniature](/wiki/Persian_miniature "Persian miniature") of Maryam in labor shaking a date palm, with Isa and hidden voice below
