Mary was declared (uniquely along with Jesus) to be a "Sign of God" to humanity;[[237]](#cite_note-246) as one who "guarded her chastity";[[50]](#cite_note-qref|66|12|b=y-55) an "obedient one";[[50]](#cite_note-qref|66|12|b=y-55) and dedicated by her mother to Allah whilst still in the womb;[[51]](#cite_note-qref|3|36|b=y-56) uniquely (amongst women) "Accepted into service by God";[[238]](#cite_note-qref|3|37|b=y-247) cared for by (one of the prophets according to Islam) [Zakariya](/wiki/Islamic_view_of_Zechariah "Islamic view of Zechariah") (Zacharias);[[238]](#cite_note-qref|3|37|b=y-247) that in her childhood she resided in the Temple and uniquely had access to Al-[Mihrab](/wiki/Mihrab "Mihrab") (understood to be the [Holy of Holies](/wiki/Holy_of_Holies "Holy of Holies")), and was provided with heavenly "provisions" by God.[[238]](#cite_note-qref|3|37|b=y-247)[[235]](#cite_note-Glasse-2003-243)

Mary is also called a "Chosen One";[[239]](#cite_note-qref|3|42|b=y-248) a "Purified One";[[239]](#cite_note-qref|3|42|b=y-248) a "Truthful one";[[240]](#cite_note-249) her child conceived through "a Word from God";[[241]](#cite_note-250) and "chosen you above the women of the worlds(the material and heavenly worlds)".[[239]](#cite_note-qref|3|42|b=y-248)

The Quran relates detailed narrative accounts of Maryam (Mary) in two places, [3:35-47](https://quran.com/3?startingVerse=35) and [19:16-34](https://quran.com/19?startingVerse=16). These state beliefs in both the Immaculate Conception of Mary and the virgin birth of Jesus.[[242]](#cite_note-251)[[243]](#cite_note-252)[[244]](#cite_note-253) The account given in [Surah Maryam](/wiki/Maryam_(surah) "Maryam (surah)") [19](https://quran.com/19?startingVerse=) is nearly identical with that in the Gospel according to [Luke](/wiki/Luke_the_Evangelist "Luke the Evangelist"), and both of these (Luke, Sura 19) begin with an account of the visitation of an angel upon Zakariya (Zecharias) and "Good News of the birth of Yahya (John)", followed by the account of the annunciation. It mentions how Mary was informed by an angel that she would become the mother of Jesus through the actions of God alone.[[245]](#cite_note-254)

In the Islamic tradition, Mary and Jesus were the only children who could not be touched by Satan at the moment of their birth, for God imposed a veil between them and Satan.[[246]](#cite_note-255)[[247]](#cite_note-256) According to the author [Shabbir Akhtar](/wiki/Shabbir_Akhtar "Shabbir Akhtar"), the Islamic perspective on Mary's Immaculate Conception is compatible with the Catholic doctrine of the same topic.

> "O [People of the Book](/wiki/People_of_the_Book "People of the Book")! Do not go to extremes regarding your faith; say nothing about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, son of Mary, was no more than a messenger of Allah and the fulfilment of His Word through Mary and a spirit ˹created by a command˺ from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers and do not say, "Trinity." Stop!—for your own good. Allah is only One God. Glory be to Him! He is far above having a son! To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And Allah is sufficient as a Trustee of Affairs.

— [Surah An-Nisa](/wiki/An-Nisa "An-Nisa") [4:171](https://quran.com/4?startingVerse=171)[[248]](#cite_note-257)[[249]](#cite_note-258)

The Quran says that Jesus was the result of a virgin birth. The most detailed account of the annunciation and birth of Jesus is provided in Suras 3 and 19 of the Quran, where it is written that God sent an angel to announce that she could shortly expect to bear a son, despite being a virgin.[[250]](#cite_note-259)

### Druze Faith

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/The_icon_of_Saydet_el_Talleh.jpg/250px-The_icon_of_Saydet_el_Talleh.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_icon_of_Saydet_el_Talleh.jpg)

The icon of [Saidet et Tallé](/wiki/Church_of_Saidet_et_Tall%C3%A9 "Church of Saidet et Tallé"), also known as "the Virgin of the Druze", is venerated by both the [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") and [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian") communities in Lebanon.[[14]](#cite_note-Bowman-2012-16)

The [Druze faith](/wiki/Druze_faith "Druze faith") holds the Virgin Mary, known as *Sayyida Maryam*, in high regard.[[128]](#cite_note-Makdisi-2000-135) Although the [Druze religion](/wiki/Druze_religion "Druze religion") is distinct from mainstream [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), it incorporates elements from both and honors many of their figures, including the Virgin Mary.[[128]](#cite_note-Makdisi-2000-135) The Druze revere Mary as a holy and pure figure, embodying virtue and piety.[[251]](#cite_note-Dana-2008-260)[[128]](#cite_note-Makdisi-2000-135) She is respected not only for her role as the mother of Messiah Jesus but also for her spiritual purity and dedication to God.[[251]](#cite_note-Dana-2008-260)[[128]](#cite_note-Makdisi-2000-135) In regions where [Druze and Christians coexist](/wiki/Christianity_and_Druze "Christianity and Druze"), such as parts of [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), the [veneration of Mary](/wiki/Marian_devotions "Marian devotions") often reflects a blend of traditions.[[252]](#cite_note-Munro-2019-261) Shared pilgrimage sites and mutual respect for places like the [Church of Saidet et Tallé](/wiki/Church_of_Saidet_et_Tall%C3%A9 "Church of Saidet et Tallé") in [Deir el Qamar](/wiki/Deir_el_Qamar "Deir el Qamar"),[[253]](#cite_note-262) the [Our Lady of Lebanon](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Lebanon "Our Lady of Lebanon") shrine in [Harrisa](/wiki/Harissa-Daraoun "Harissa-Daraoun"), the [Our Lady of Saidnaya Monastery](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Saidnaya_Monastery "Our Lady of Saidnaya Monastery") in [Saidnaya](/wiki/Saidnaya "Saidnaya"), and the [Stella Maris Monastery](/wiki/Stella_Maris_Monastery "Stella Maris Monastery") in [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa") exemplify this.[[252]](#cite_note-Munro-2019-261)

Historical records and writings by authors like Pierre-Marie Martin and Glenn Bowman show that Druze leaders and community members have historically shown deep reverence for [Marian sites](/wiki/Shrines_to_the_Virgin_Mary "Shrines to the Virgin Mary").[[14]](#cite_note-Bowman-2012-16) They often sought her intercession before battles or during times of need, demonstrating a cultural and spiritual integration of Marian veneration into their religious practices.[[14]](#cite_note-Bowman-2012-16)

### Bahá'í Faith

The [Bahá'í Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_Faith "Bahá'í Faith") venerates Mary as the mother of Jesus. The *[Kitáb-i-Íqán](/wiki/Kit%C3%A1b-i-%C3%8Dq%C3%A1n "Kitáb-i-Íqán")*, the primary theological work of the Bahá'í religion, describes Mary as "that most beauteous countenance," and "that veiled and immortal Countenance." The Bahá'í writings claim Jesus Christ was "conceived of the Holy Ghost"[[254]](#cite_note-263) and assert that in the [Bahá'í Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_Faith "Bahá'í Faith") "the reality of the mystery of the Immaculacy of the Virgin Mary is confessed."[[255]](#cite_note-264)

### Biblical scholars

The statement found in [Matthew 1:25](/wiki/Matthew_1:25 "Matthew 1:25") that [Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph") did not have sexual relations with Mary before she gave birth to Jesus has been debated among scholars, with some saying that she did not remain a virgin and some saying that she was a perpetual virgin.[[256]](#cite_note-265) Other scholars contend that the Greek word *heos* ("until") denotes a state up to a point, but does not mean that the state ended after that point, and that Matthew 1:25 does not confirm or deny the virginity of Mary after the birth of Jesus.[[257]](#cite_note-266)[[258]](#cite_note-267)[[259]](#cite_note-268) According to Biblical scholar [Bart Ehrman](/wiki/Bart_Ehrman "Bart Ehrman"), the Hebrew word *[almah](/wiki/Almah "Almah")*, meaning young woman of childbearing age, was translated into Greek as *parthenos*, which often, though not always, refers to a young woman who has never had sexual intercourse. In Isaiah 7:14, it is commonly believed by Christians to be the prophecy of the Virgin Mary referred to in Matthew 1:23.[[260]](#cite_note-269) While Matthew and Luke give differing versions of the virgin birth, John quotes the uninitiated Philip and the disbelieving Jews gathered at Galilee referring to Joseph as Jesus' father.[[261]](#cite_note-270)[[262]](#cite_note-271)[[263]](#cite_note-272)[[264]](#cite_note-273)

Other biblical verses have also been debated; for example, the reference made by Paul the Apostle that Jesus was made "of the seed of David according to the flesh" (Romans 1:3)[[265]](#cite_note-274) meaning that he was a descendant of David through Joseph.[[266]](#cite_note-275)

### Pre-Christian Rome

From the early stages of Christianity, belief in the virginity of Mary and the virgin conception of Jesus, as stated in the gospels, holy and supernatural, was used by detractors, both political and religious, as a topic for discussions, debates, and writings, specifically aimed to challenge the divinity of Jesus and thus Christians and Christianity alike.[[267]](#cite_note-276) In the 2nd century, as part of his [anti-Christian](/wiki/Anti-Christian "Anti-Christian") polemic *[The True Word](/wiki/The_True_Word "The True Word")*, the pagan philosopher [Celsus](/wiki/Celsus "Celsus") contended that Jesus was actually the illegitimate son of a Roman soldier named [Panthera](/wiki/Tiberius_Julius_Abdes_Pantera "Tiberius Julius Abdes Pantera").[[268]](#cite_note-277) The Church Father [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen") dismissed this assertion as a complete fabrication in his apologetic treatise *[Against Celsus](/wiki/Contra_Celsum "Contra Celsum")*.[[269]](#cite_note-278) How far Celsus sourced his view from Jewish sources remains a subject of discussion.[[270]](#cite_note-279)

## Christian devotions

Main article: [Marian devotions](/wiki/Marian_devotions "Marian devotions")

### History

#### 2nd century

[Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") was among the first to draw a [parallel between Eve and Mary](/wiki/New_Eve "New Eve"). This derives from his comparison of Adam and Jesus. In his *[Dialogue with Trypho](/wiki/Dialogue_with_Trypho "Dialogue with Trypho")*, written sometime between 155 and 167,[[271]](#cite_note-280) he explains:

> He became man by the Virgin, in order that the disobedience which proceeded from the serpent might receive its destruction in the same manner in which it derived its origin. For Eve, who was a virgin and undefiled, having conceived the word of the serpent, brought forth disobedience and death. But the Virgin Mary received faith and joy, when the angel Gabriel announced the good tidings to her that the Spirit of the Lord would come upon her, and the power of the Highest would overshadow her: wherefore also the Holy Thing begotten of her is the Son of God; and she replied, 'Be it unto me according to thy word." And by her has He been born, to whom we have proved so many scriptures refer, and by whom God destroys both the serpent and those angels and men who are like him; but works deliverance from death to those who repent of their wickedness and believe upon Him.[[272]](#cite_note-281)

It is possible that the teaching of Mary as the New Eve was part of the apostolic tradition rather than merely Justin Martyr's own creation, according to [Serafim Seppälä](/wiki/Serafim_Sepp%C3%A4l%C3%A4 "Serafim Seppälä").[[273]](#cite_note-282) [Irenaeus](/wiki/Irenaeus "Irenaeus"), [bishop of Lyon](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lyon "Bishop of Lyon"), also takes up this parallel, in *Against Heresies*, written about the year 182:[[274]](#cite_note-283)

> In accordance with this design, Mary the Virgin is found obedient, saying, "Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to your word." Luke 1:38 But Eve was disobedient; for she did not obey when as yet she was a virgin. ... having become disobedient, was made the cause of death, both to herself and to the entire human race; so also did Mary, having a man betrothed [to her], and being nevertheless a virgin, by yielding obedience, become the cause of salvation, both to herself and the whole human race. And on this account does the law term a woman betrothed to a man, the wife of him who had betrothed her, although she was as yet a virgin; thus indicating the back-reference from Mary to Eve,...For the Lord, having been born "the First-begotten of the dead," Revelation 1:5 and receiving into His bosom the ancient fathers, has regenerated them into the life of God, He having been made Himself the beginning of those that live, as Adam became the beginning of those who die. 1 Corinthians 15:20–22 Wherefore also Luke, commencing the genealogy with the Lord, carried it back to Adam, indicating that it was He who regenerated them into the Gospel of life, and not they Him. And thus also it was that the knot of Eve's disobedience was loosed by the obedience of Mary. For what the virgin Eve had bound fast through unbelief, this did the virgin Mary set free through faith.[[275]](#cite_note-284)

During the second century, the Gospel of James was also written. According to [Stephen J. Shoemaker](/wiki/Stephen_J._Shoemaker "Stephen J. Shoemaker"), "its interest in Mary as a figure in her own right and its reverence for her sacred purity mark the beginnings of Marian piety within early Christianity".[[276]](#cite_note-285)

#### 3rd to 5th centuries

During the Age of Martyrs and at the latest in the fourth century, the majority of the most essential ideas of Marian devotion already appeared in some form – in the writings of the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers"), apocrypha and visual arts. The lack of sources makes it unclear whether the devotion to Mary played a role in liturgical use during the first centuries of Christianity.[[277]](#cite_note-286) In the 4th century, Marian devotion in a liturgical context becomes evident.[[278]](#cite_note-287)

The earliest known Marian prayer (the *[Sub tuum praesidium](/wiki/Sub_tuum_praesidium "Sub tuum praesidium")*, or *Beneath Thy Protection*) is from the 3rd century (perhaps 270), and its text was rediscovered in 1917 on a papyrus in Egypt.[[279]](#cite_note-288)[[280]](#cite_note-289) According to some sources, [Theonas of Alexandria](/wiki/Pope_Theonas_of_Alexandria "Pope Theonas of Alexandria") consecrated one of the first holy places dedicated to Mary during the late 3rd century. An even earlier place has been found in Nazareth, dated to the previous century by some scholars.[[281]](#cite_note-290) Following the [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan") in 313, by the 5th century artistic images of Mary began to appear in public and larger churches were being dedicated to Mary, such as the [Basilica di Santa Maria Maggiore](/wiki/Basilica_di_Santa_Maria_Maggiore "Basilica di Santa Maria Maggiore") in Rome.[[282]](#cite_note-291)[[283]](#cite_note-292)[[284]](#cite_note-293) At the [Council of Ephesus](/wiki/Council_of_Ephesus "Council of Ephesus") in 431, Mary was officially declared the *[Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos")*, meaning "God-bearer"[[285]](#cite_note-294) or "Mother of God". The term had possibly been used for centuries[[286]](#cite_note-295) or at least since the early 300s, when it seems to have already been in established use.[[287]](#cite_note-296)

The Council of Ephesus was long thought to have been held at a church in Ephesus which had been dedicated to Mary about a hundred years before.[[288]](#cite_note-297)[[289]](#cite_note-298)[[290]](#cite_note-299) Though, recent archeological surveys indicate that St. Mary's Church in Ephesus did not exist at the time of the Council or, at least, the building was not dedicated to Mary before 500.[[291]](#cite_note-300) The [Church of the Seat of Mary](/wiki/Church_of_the_Seat_of_Mary "Church of the Seat of Mary") in [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea") was built shortly after the introduction of Marian liturgy at the council of Ephesus, in 456, by a widow named Ikelia.[[292]](#cite_note-301)

According to the 4th-century heresiologist [Epiphanius of Salamis](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis"), the Virgin Mary was worshipped as a [mother goddess](/wiki/Mother_goddess "Mother goddess") in the Christian sect of [Collyridianism](/wiki/Collyridianism "Collyridianism"), which was found throughout Arabia sometime during the 300s AD. Collyridianism had women performing priestly acts, and made bread offerings to the Virgin Mary. The group was condemned as heretical by the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") and was preached against by [Epiphanius of Salamis](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis"), who wrote about the group in his writings titled [Panarion](/wiki/Panarion "Panarion").[[293]](#cite_note-302)

#### Byzantium

During the era of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), Mary was venerated as the virginal Mother of God and as an intercessor.[[294]](#cite_note-303)

[Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus") is a cultic centre of Mary, the site of the first church dedicated to her and the rumoured place of her death. Ephesus was previously a centre for worship of [Artemis](/wiki/Artemis "Artemis"), a virgin goddess; the [Temple of Artemis](/wiki/Temple_of_Artemis "Temple of Artemis") there is regarded as one of the [Seven Wonders of the Ancient World](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_the_Ancient_World "Seven Wonders of the Ancient World"). The cult of Mary was furthered by [Queen Theodora](/wiki/Theodora_(wife_of_Justinian_I) "Theodora (wife of Justinian I)") in the 6th century.[[295]](#cite_note-304)[[296]](#cite_note-305) According to William E. Phipps, in the book *Survivals of Roman Religion*,[[297]](#cite_note-306) "Gordon Laing argues convincingly that the worship of Artemis as both virgin and mother at the grand Ephesian temple contributed to the veneration of Mary."[[298]](#cite_note-307)

#### Middle Ages

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Angelico%2C_madonna_col_bambino%2C_pinacoteca_sabauda.jpg/250px-Angelico%2C_madonna_col_bambino%2C_pinacoteca_sabauda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Angelico,_madonna_col_bambino,_pinacoteca_sabauda.jpg)

*[Madonna of humility](/wiki/Madonna_of_humility "Madonna of humility")* by [Fra Angelico](/wiki/Fra_Angelico "Fra Angelico"), c. 1430. A traditional depiction of Mary wearing blue clothes.

The Middle Ages saw many legends about Mary, her parents, and even her grandparents.[[299]](#cite_note-308) Mary's popularity increased dramatically from the 12th century,[[300]](#cite_note-309) linked to the Roman Catholic Church's designation of Mary as [Mediatrix](/wiki/Mediatrix "Mediatrix").[[301]](#cite_note-310)[[302]](#cite_note-311)

#### Post-Reformation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/The_Madonna_in_Sorrow.jpg/330px-The_Madonna_in_Sorrow.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Madonna_in_Sorrow.jpg)

*Our Lady of Sorrows* by [Giovanni Battista Salvi da Sassoferrato](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Salvi_da_Sassoferrato "Giovanni Battista Salvi da Sassoferrato"), 17th century

Over the centuries, devotion and veneration to Mary has varied greatly among Christian traditions. For instance, while Protestants show scant attention to Marian prayers or devotions, of all the saints whom the Orthodox venerate, the most honored is Mary, who is considered "more honorable than the [Cherubim](/wiki/Cherubim "Cherubim") and more glorious than the [Seraphim](/wiki/Seraphim "Seraphim")".[[31]](#cite_note-Fairbairn-2002-35)

Orthodox theologian [Sergei Bulgakov](/wiki/Sergei_Bulgakov "Sergei Bulgakov") wrote: "Love and veneration for the Virgin is the soul of Orthodox piety. A faith in Christ which does not include [...] His mother is another faith, another Christianity from that of the Orthodox Church."[[176]](#cite_note-Bulgakov-1997-183)

Although the Catholics and the Orthodox may honor and venerate Mary, they do not view her as divine, nor do they worship her. Roman Catholics view Mary as subordinate to Christ, but uniquely so, in that she is seen as above all other creatures.[[303]](#cite_note-312) Similarly, Bulgakov wrote that the Orthodox view Mary as "superior to all created beings" and "ceaselessly pray for her intercession". However, she is not considered a "substitute for the One Mediator" who is Christ.[[176]](#cite_note-Bulgakov-1997-183) "Let Mary be in honor, but let worship be given to the Lord", he wrote.[[304]](#cite_note-313) Similarly, Catholics do not worship Mary as a divine being, but rather "hyper-venerate" her. In Roman Catholic theology, the term *[hyperdulia](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration")* is reserved for Marian veneration, *[latria](/wiki/Latria "Latria")* for the worship of God, and *[dulia](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration")* for the veneration of other saints and angels.[[305]](#cite_note-314) The definition of the three level hierarchy of *latria*, *hyperdulia* and *dulia* goes back to the [Second Council of Nicaea](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Nicaea "Second Council of Nicaea") in 787.[[306]](#cite_note-315)

Devotions to artistic depictions of Mary vary among Christian traditions. There is a long tradition of [Catholic Marian art](/wiki/Catholic_Marian_art "Catholic Marian art") and no image permeates [Catholic art](/wiki/Catholic_art "Catholic art") as does the image of [Madonna and Child](/wiki/Madonna_and_Child "Madonna and Child").[[307]](#cite_note-316) The icon of the Virgin *Theotokos* with Christ is, without doubt, the most venerated icon in the Orthodox Church.[[308]](#cite_note-317) Both Roman Catholic and Orthodox Christians venerate images and icons of Mary, given that the [Second Council of Nicaea](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Nicaea "Second Council of Nicaea") in 787 permitted their veneration with the understanding that those who venerate the image are venerating the reality of the person it represents,[[309]](#cite_note-318) and the 842 Synod of Constantinople confirming the same.[[310]](#cite_note-319) According to Orthodox piety and traditional practice, however, believers ought to pray before and venerate only flat, two-dimensional icons, and not three-dimensional statues.[[311]](#cite_note-320)

The Anglican position towards Mary is in general more conciliatory than that of Protestants at large and in a book he wrote about praying with the icons of Mary, [Rowan Williams](/wiki/Rowan_Williams "Rowan Williams"), former [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), said: "It is not only that we cannot understand Mary without seeing her as pointing to Christ; we cannot understand Christ without seeing his attention to Mary."[[130]](#cite_note-Schroedel-2006-137)[[312]](#cite_note-321)

On 4 September 1781, 11 families of *[pobladores](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Pobladores "Los Angeles Pobladores")* arrived from the Gulf of California and established a city in the name of [King Carlos III](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain"). The small town was named *El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora de los Ángeles de la Porciúncula* (after our Lady of the Angels), a city that today is known simply as [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). In an attempt to revive the custom of religious processions within the [Archdiocese of Los Angeles](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Los_Angeles "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Los Angeles"), in September 2011 the [Queen of Angels Foundation](/wiki/Queen_of_Angels_Foundation "Queen of Angels Foundation"), and founder Mark Anchor Albert, inaugurated an annual Grand Marian Procession in the heart of [Downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles")' historic core. This yearly procession, held on the last Saturday of August and intended to coincide with the anniversary of the founding of the City of Los Angeles, begins at the [Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Angels "Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels") and concludes at the parish of [La Iglesia de Nuestra Señora Reina de los Angeles](/wiki/La_Iglesia_de_Nuestra_Se%C3%B1ora_Reina_de_los_Angeles "La Iglesia de Nuestra Señora Reina de los Angeles") which is part of the [Los Angeles Plaza Historic District](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Plaza_Historic_District "Los Angeles Plaza Historic District"), better known as "La Placita".[[313]](#cite_note-322)[[314]](#cite_note-323)

### Feasts

Main article: [Marian feast days](/wiki/Marian_feast_days "Marian feast days")

The earliest feasts that relate to Mary grew out of the cycle of feasts that celebrated the [Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus"). Given that according to the Gospel of Luke (Luke 2:22–40),[[315]](#cite_note-324) 40 days after the birth of Jesus, along with the [Presentation of Jesus at the Temple](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple"), Mary was purified according to Jewish customs. The Feast of the Purification began to be celebrated by the 5th century, and became the "Feast of [Simeon](/wiki/Simeon_(Gospel_of_Luke) "Simeon (Gospel of Luke)")" in [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium").[[316]](#cite_note-Clayton-2003-325)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Pjazzasantamarijaghaxaqmalta.JPG/250px-Pjazzasantamarijaghaxaqmalta.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pjazzasantamarijaghaxaqmalta.JPG)

Village decorations during the *[Feast of the Assumption](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Assumption "Feast of the Assumption")* in [Għaxaq](/wiki/G%C4%A7axaq "Għaxaq"), Malta

In the 7th and 8th centuries, four more Marian feasts were established in [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"). In the [West](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), a feast dedicated to Mary, just before Christmas was celebrated in the Churches of [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") in Italy in the 7th century. The four Roman Marian feasts of Purification, Annunciation, Assumption and Nativity of Mary were gradually and sporadically introduced into England by the 11th century.[[316]](#cite_note-Clayton-2003-325)

Over time, the number and nature of feasts (and the associated [Titles of Mary](/wiki/Titles_of_Mary "Titles of Mary")) and the venerative practices that accompany them have varied a great deal among diverse Christian traditions. Overall, there are significantly more titles, feasts and venerative Marian practices among [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics") than any other Christians traditions.[[129]](#cite_note-Flinn-2007-136) Some such feasts relate to specific events, such as the Feast of [Our Lady of Victory](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Victory "Our Lady of Victory"), based on the 1571 victory of the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States") in the [Battle of Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto_(1571) "Battle of Lepanto (1571)").[[317]](#cite_note-326)[[318]](#cite_note-327)

Differences in feasts may also originate from doctrinal issues—the [Feast of the Assumption](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Assumption "Feast of the Assumption") is such an example. Given that there is no agreement among all Christians on the circumstances of the death, [Dormition](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Theotokos "Dormition of the Theotokos") or Assumption of Mary, the feast of assumption is celebrated among some denominations and not others.[[30]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2003-34)[[319]](#cite_note-328) While the Roman Catholic Church celebrates the Feast of the Assumption on 15 August, some [Eastern Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") celebrate it as [Dormition of the *Theotokos*](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Mother_of_God "Dormition of the Mother of God"), and may do so on 28 August, if they follow the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"). The [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") also celebrate it as the Dormition of the *Theotokos*, one of their 12 [Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts_of_the_Orthodox_Church "Great Feasts of the Orthodox Church"). Protestants do not celebrate this, or any other Marian feasts.[[30]](#cite_note-Hillerbrand-2003-34)

### Relics

The veneration of Marian [relics](/wiki/Relics "Relics") used to be common practice before the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"). It was later largely surpassed by the veneration of Marian images.

#### Bodily relics

As Mary's body is believed by most Christians to have been [taken up into the glory of heaven](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary"), her bodily relics have been limited to hair, nails and [breast milk](/wiki/Nursing_Madonna "Nursing Madonna").

According to [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin")'s 1543 *[Treatise on Relics](/wiki/Treatise_on_Relics "Treatise on Relics")*, her hair was exposed for veneration in several churches, including in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Saint-Flour](/wiki/Saint-Flour "Saint-Flour"), [Cluny](/wiki/Cluny "Cluny") and [Nevers](/wiki/Nevers "Nevers").[[320]](#cite_note-329)

In this book, Calvin criticized the veneration of the Holy Milk due to the lack of biblical references to it and the doubts about the veracity of such relics:

> With regard to the milk, there is not perhaps a town, a convent, or nunnery, where it is not shown in large or small quantities. Indeed, had the Virgin been a wet-nurse her whole life, or a dairy, she could not have produced more than is shown as hers in various parts. How they obtained all this milk they do not say, and it is superfluous here to remark that there is no foundation in the Gospels for these foolish and blasphemous extravagances.

Although the veneration of Marian bodily relics is no longer a common practice today, there are some remaining traces of it, such as the [Chapel of the Milk Grotto](/wiki/Chapel_of_the_Milk_Grotto "Chapel of the Milk Grotto") in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"), named after Mary's milk.

#### Clothes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Syriac_Orthodox_Holy_Girdle.jpg/250px-Syriac_Orthodox_Holy_Girdle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Syriac_Orthodox_Holy_Girdle.jpg)

Late His Eminence Meletios Barnabas, Former [Syriac Orthodox Arch Bishop of Homs](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Archdiocese_of_Homs "Syriac Orthodox Archdiocese of Homs"), with the [Holy Girdle](/wiki/Holy_Girdle "Holy Girdle") of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary")

Clothes which are believed to have belonged to Mary include the [Cincture of the Theotokos](/wiki/Cincture_of_the_Theotokos "Cincture of the Theotokos") kept in the [Vatopedi monastery](/wiki/Vatopedi_monastery "Vatopedi monastery") and her [Holy Girdle](/wiki/Holy_Girdle "Holy Girdle") kept in [Holy Belt Church, Homs](/wiki/Saint_Mary_Church_of_the_Holy_Belt "Saint Mary Church of the Holy Belt").

Other relics are said to have been collected during later [Marian apparitions](/wiki/Marian_apparitions "Marian apparitions"), such as her robe, veil, and part of her belt which were kept in [Blachernae church](/wiki/Church_of_St._Mary_of_Blachernae_(Istanbul) "Church of St. Mary of Blachernae (Istanbul)") in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") after she appeared there during the 10th century. These relics, now lost, are celebrated by the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") and [Byzantine Catholic Churches](/wiki/Byzantine_Catholic_Church "Byzantine Catholic Church") as the [Intercession of the Theotokos](/wiki/Intercession_of_the_Theotokos "Intercession of the Theotokos").

Few other objects are said to have been touched or given by Mary during [apparitions](/wiki/Marian_apparitions "Marian apparitions"), notably a 1531 image printed on a *[tilma](/wiki/Tilm%C3%A0tli "Tilmàtli")*, known as [Our Lady of Guadalupe](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Guadalupe "Our Lady of Guadalupe"), belonging to [Juan Diego](/wiki/Juan_Diego "Juan Diego").

#### Places

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/House_of_the_Virgin_Mary.jpg/250px-House_of_the_Virgin_Mary.jpg)](/wiki/File:House_of_the_Virgin_Mary.jpg)

The chapel based on the claimed [House of Mary](/wiki/House_of_Mary "House of Mary") in Ephesus

Places where Mary is believed to have lived include the [Basilica della Santa Casa](/wiki/Basilica_della_Santa_Casa "Basilica della Santa Casa") in [Loreto, Marche](/wiki/Loreto,_Marche "Loreto, Marche"), and the [House of the Virgin Mary](/wiki/House_of_the_Virgin_Mary "House of the Virgin Mary") in [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus").

[Eastern Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Christians "Eastern Christians") believe that she died and was put in the [Tomb of the Virgin Mary](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Virgin_Mary "Tomb of the Virgin Mary") near [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") before the [Assumption](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary").

The belief that Mary's house was in [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus") is recent, as it was claimed in the 19th century based on the visions of [Anne Catherine Emmerich](/wiki/Anne_Catherine_Emmerich "Anne Catherine Emmerich"), an [Augustinian nun](/wiki/Augustinian_nun "Augustinian nun") in Germany.[[321]](#cite_note-330)[[322]](#cite_note-331) It has since been named as the [House of the Virgin Mary](/wiki/House_of_the_Virgin_Mary "House of the Virgin Mary") by [Roman Catholic pilgrims](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage") who consider it the place where Mary lived until her assumption.[[323]](#cite_note-332)[[324]](#cite_note-333)[[325]](#cite_note-334)[[326]](#cite_note-335) The Gospel of John states that Mary went to live with the [Disciple whom Jesus loved](/wiki/Disciple_whom_Jesus_loved "Disciple whom Jesus loved"),[[327]](#cite_note-336) [traditionally identified](/wiki/Disciple_whom_Jesus_loved#Identity "Disciple whom Jesus loved") as [John the Evangelist](/wiki/John_the_Evangelist "John the Evangelist")[[328]](#cite_note-337) and [John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle"). [Irenaeus](/wiki/Irenaeus "Irenaeus") and [Eusebius of Caesarea](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Caesarea "Eusebius of Caesarea") wrote in their histories that John later went to Ephesus, which may provide the basis for the early belief that Mary also lived in Ephesus with John.[[329]](#cite_note-338)[[330]](#cite_note-339)

The apparition of [Our Lady of the Pillar](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_the_Pillar "Our Lady of the Pillar") in the first century was believed to be a [bilocation](/wiki/Bilocation "Bilocation"), as it occurred in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") while Mary was living in Ephesus or Jerusalem. The pillar on which she was standing during the apparition is believed to be kept in the [Cathedral-Basilica of Our Lady of the Pillar](/wiki/Cathedral-Basilica_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Pillar "Cathedral-Basilica of Our Lady of the Pillar") in [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza") and is therefore venerated as a relics, as it was in physical contact with Mary.

## In arts

### Iconography

See also: [Madonna (art)](/wiki/Madonna_(art) "Madonna (art)") and [Marian art in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Marian_art_in_the_Catholic_Church "Marian art in the Catholic Church")

In paintings, Mary is traditionally portrayed in [blue](/wiki/Marian_blue "Marian blue"). This tradition can trace its origin to the Byzantine Empire, from c. 500 AD, where blue was "the colour of an empress". A more practical explanation for the use of this colour is that in Medieval and Renaissance Europe, the blue pigment was derived from the rock [lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli"), a stone of greater value than gold, which was imported from Afghanistan. Beyond a painter's retainer, patrons were expected to purchase any gold or lapis lazuli to be used in the painting. Hence, it was an expression of devotion and glorification to swathe the Virgin in gowns of blue. Transformations in visual depictions of Mary from the 13th to 15th centuries mirror her "social" standing within the Church and in society.[[331]](#cite_note-340)

Traditional representations of Mary include the [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") scene, called *[Stabat Mater](/wiki/Stabat_Mater_(art) "Stabat Mater (art)")*.[[332]](#cite_note-341)[[333]](#cite_note-342) While not recorded in the Gospel accounts, Mary cradling the dead body of her son is a common motif in art, called a "[pietà](/wiki/Piet%C3%A0 "Pietà")" or "pity".

In the Egyptian, Eritrean, and Ethiopian tradition, Mary has been portrayed in story and paint for centuries.[[334]](#cite_note-343) Beginning in the 1600s, however, highland Ethiopians began portraying Mary performing a variety of miracles for the faithful, including paintings of her giving water to a thirsty dog, healing monks with her breast milk, and saving a man eaten by a crocodile.[[335]](#cite_note-344) Over 1,000 such [stories](https://pemm.princeton.edu/stories) about her exist in this tradition, and about one hundred of those have hundreds of paintings each, in various manuscripts, adding up to thousands of [paintings](https://pemm.princeton.edu/paintings).[[336]](#cite_note-345)

For a larger gallery, see [Commons:Madonna structured gallery](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Madonna_structured_gallery "commons:Madonna structured gallery").

### Cinematic portrayals

Mary has been portrayed in various films and on television, including:

* *[The Miracle](/wiki/The_Miracle_(1912_film) "The Miracle (1912 film)")* (1912) color [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") of the 1911 play *[The Miracle](/wiki/The_Miracle_(play) "The Miracle (play)")*, a statue of Mary, played by [Maria Carmi](/wiki/Norina_Matchabelli "Norina Matchabelli"), comes to life
* *[Das Mirakel](/wiki/Das_Mirakel_(1912_film) "Das Mirakel (1912 film)")* (1912) [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film"); a German version of the 1911 play *[The Miracle](/wiki/The_Miracle_(play) "The Miracle (play)")*
* *[The Song of Bernadette](/wiki/The_Song_of_Bernadette_(film) "The Song of Bernadette (film)")* (1943 film), played by [Linda Darnell](/wiki/Linda_Darnell "Linda Darnell").
* *[The Living Christ Series](/wiki/The_Living_Christ_Series "The Living Christ Series")* (1951 non-theatrical, non-television film twelve-part series), played by [Eileen Rowe](/w/index.php?title=Eileen_Rowe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eileen Rowe (page does not exist)").
* *[The Miracle of Our Lady of Fatima](/wiki/The_Miracle_of_Our_Lady_of_Fatima "The Miracle of Our Lady of Fatima")* (1952 film), played by [Virginia Gibson](/wiki/Virginia_Gibson "Virginia Gibson").
* *[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1959_film) "Ben-Hur (1959 film)")* (1959 film), played by [José Greci](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Greci "José Greci").[[337]](#cite_note-346)
* *[The Miracle](/wiki/The_Miracle_(1959_film) "The Miracle (1959 film)")* (1959 film; a loose remake of the 1912 film *[Das Mirakel](/wiki/Das_Mirakel_(1912_film) "Das Mirakel (1912 film)")*)
* *[King of Kings](/wiki/King_of_Kings_(1961_film) "King of Kings (1961 film)")* (1961 film), played by [Siobhán McKenna](/wiki/Siobh%C3%A1n_McKenna "Siobhán McKenna").[[338]](#cite_note-347)
* *[The Greatest Story Ever Told](/wiki/The_Greatest_Story_Ever_Told "The Greatest Story Ever Told")* (1965 film), played by [Dorothy McGuire](/wiki/Dorothy_McGuire "Dorothy McGuire").[[339]](#cite_note-348)
* *[Jesus of Nazareth](/wiki/Jesus_of_Nazareth_(miniseries) "Jesus of Nazareth (miniseries)")* (1977 two-part television miniseries), played by [Olivia Hussey](/wiki/Olivia_Hussey "Olivia Hussey").[[340]](#cite_note-349)
* *[The Last Temptation of Christ](/wiki/The_Last_Temptation_of_Christ_(film) "The Last Temptation of Christ (film)")* (1988 film), played by [Verna Bloom](/wiki/Verna_Bloom "Verna Bloom").[[341]](#cite_note-350)
* *[Mary, Mother of Jesus](/wiki/Mary,_Mother_of_Jesus_(film) "Mary, Mother of Jesus (film)")* (1999 television film), played by [Pernilla August](/wiki/Pernilla_August "Pernilla August").[[342]](#cite_note-351)
* *[Saint Mary](/wiki/Saint_Mary_(film) "Saint Mary (film)")* (2001 film), played by [Shabnam Gholikhani](/wiki/Shabnam_Gholikhani "Shabnam Gholikhani").[[343]](#cite_note-352)
* *[The Passion of the Christ](/wiki/The_Passion_of_the_Christ "The Passion of the Christ")* (2004 film), played by [Maia Morgenstern](/wiki/Maia_Morgenstern "Maia Morgenstern").[[344]](#cite_note-353)
* *[Imperium: Saint Peter](/wiki/Imperium:_Saint_Peter "Imperium: Saint Peter")* (2005 television film), played by [Lina Sastri](/wiki/Lina_Sastri "Lina Sastri").
* *[Color of the Cross](/wiki/Color_of_the_Cross "Color of the Cross")* (2006 film), played by [Debbi Morgan](/wiki/Debbi_Morgan "Debbi Morgan").[[345]](#cite_note-354)
* *[The Nativity Story](/wiki/The_Nativity_Story "The Nativity Story")* (2006 film), played by [Keisha Castle-Hughes](/wiki/Keisha_Castle-Hughes "Keisha Castle-Hughes").[[346]](#cite_note-355)
* *[The Passion](/wiki/The_Passion_(TV_serial) "The Passion (TV serial)")* (2008 television miniseries), played by [Paloma Baeza](/wiki/Paloma_Baeza "Paloma Baeza").[[347]](#cite_note-356)[[348]](#cite_note-357)
* *[The Nativity](/wiki/The_Nativity_(2010_TV_series) "The Nativity (2010 TV series)")* (2010 four-part miniseries), played by [Tatiana Maslany](/wiki/Tatiana_Maslany "Tatiana Maslany").

* *[Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God_(film) "Son of God (film)")* (2014 film), played by [Roma Downey](/wiki/Roma_Downey "Roma Downey").[[349]](#cite_note-358)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** Per the [Jewish customs surrounding marriage](/wiki/Bar_and_bat_mitzvah#Significance "Bar and bat mitzvah") at the time, and the apocryphal [Gospel of James](/wiki/Gospel_of_James "Gospel of James"), Mary, at the time of her betrothal to Joseph, was 12–14 years old.[[1]](#cite_note-NewAdvent-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Her year of birth is therefore contingent on [that of Jesus](/wiki/Date_of_birth_of_Jesus "Date of birth of Jesus"), and though some posit slightly different dates (such as [Meier](/wiki/John_P._Meier "John P. Meier")'s dating of c. 7 or 6 BC)[[5]](#cite_note-5) general consensus places Jesus' birth in c. 4 BC,[[6]](#cite_note-6) thus placing Mary's birth in c. 18 BC.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): מִרְיָם, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *Mīryām*; [Classical Syriac](/wiki/Classical_Syriac_language "Classical Syriac language"): ܡܪܝܡ, romanized: *Maryam*; [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): مريم, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Maryam*; [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Μαρία, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *María*; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Maria*; [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_language "Coptic language"): Ⲙⲁⲣⲓⲁ, romanized: *Maria*
3. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): παρθένος, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *"parthénos"*; Matthew 1:23[[15]](#cite_note-17) uses the Greek *parthénos*, "virgin", whereas only the Hebrew of Isaiah 7:14,[[16]](#cite_note-18) from which the New Testament ostensibly quotes, as *Almah* – "young maiden". See article on *parthénos* in Bauercc/(Arndt)/Gingrich/Danker, *A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature*.[[17]](#cite_note-19)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** See [Sabine R. Huebner](/wiki/Sabine_R._Huebner "Sabine R. Huebner")'s succinct analysis of the issue: "Jesus is described as the 'first-born son' of Mary in Mt 1:25 and Lk 2:7. From this wording alone we can conclude that there were later-born sons ... The family ... had at least five sons and an unknown number of daughters."[[27]](#cite_note-30)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-52)** To give a few examples, [Our Lady of Good Counsel](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Good_Counsel "Our Lady of Good Counsel"), [Our Lady of Navigators](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Navigators "Our Lady of Navigators"), and [Our Lady Undoer of Knots](/wiki/Mary_Untier_of_Knots "Mary Untier of Knots") fit this description.[[44]](#cite_note-48)[[45]](#cite_note-49)[[46]](#cite_note-50)[[47]](#cite_note-51)
